mechanism: When task_type is factual, scores reflect answerability from model knowledge or context and can reward text-similarity-based evaluation; when task_type is evaluation, the setup instead probes benchmark coverage or closed-book protocol properties, which can weaken or invert the apparent benefit of the same evaluation-method.
predictions: On the same benchmark, factual mode will show larger gains for evaluation-method than evaluation mode.
Holding model and metric fixed, switching only task_type to evaluation will reduce or erase the reported advantage.
minimal_test: On TruthfulQA and one benchmark from arxiv:2401.14887v4, keep model, prompts, metric, retrieval, and context fixed; sweep only task_type between factual QA answering and evaluation/meta-benchmark framing, then compare the effect size attributed to evaluation-method.

scope_conditions.task: factual-QA

EDA papers mention tool comprehension and Grade A more often than retrieval metrics

Customer service papers report reference-based generation metrics more often than astronomy retrieval papers

HyDE+rereanking improves customer service MRR on underspecified questions

scope_conditions.task: multi-document retrieval and domain-QA over text corpora

A citation-grounding intervention will reduce web action hallucinations on a subset of tasks

Science RAG retrievers adapted to web knowledge-seeking tasks will improve completion on information-heavy websites

Reasoning-trained models retain some gains when rescored on APPS or MBPP pass@k

A small-agent setup improves hard programming tasks more than large-agent setups once communication cost is controlled

Code-reasoning models exhibit uneven robustness across dialectal instructions and offensive-token rates when evaluated like programming models

Injecting safety terms into politics-paper queries retrieves uncited relevant neighbors

scope_conditions.task: text + image reliability assessment under adversarial or misleading inputs

Safety infection metrics reveal hidden cascade failures in political fact-checking pipelines

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because the communities are separated by methodological priors: safety prioritizes worst-case robustness and alignment interventions, while politics prioritizes average-case factual performance, so even active recent work does not treat the other's benchmarks as decisive.

Politics methods lose more performance under adversarial perturbations than their reported F1 suggests

hypothesis: The disagreement is driven by dataset composition: RAG helps on multimodal, event-localized long-context video QA but hurts or is unnecessary on synthetic or text-only long-context benchmarks where the answer is already recoverable from contiguous context.
mechanism: When questions depend on sparse, temporally localized evidence scattered across very long videos, retrieval increases the signal-to-noise ratio by surfacing the right segments; on synthetic/text benchmarks such as BABILong or settings comparing against full-context reading, chunk retrieval can discard cross-sentence dependencies and break compositional reasoning, flipping the sign.
predictions: RAG gains grow on sparse-evidence video subsets
RAG loses on contiguous synthetic/text subsets
minimal_test: Evaluate the same RAG pipeline and the same base model on matched subsets from long-context video understanding and BABILong/text QA, controlling for context budget and prompt format; then stratify by evidence locality (sparse dispersed vs contiguous) and check whether the contradiction shrinks or reverses within strata.

scope_conditions.task: long-context-QA across video and text benchmarks

evidence_gap: The cited claims do not report a common benchmark split annotated for evidence locality or modality, so cross-paper comparisons may conflate task family with method effect.

hypothesis: The contradiction is moderated by retrieval recall and top-k setting: RAG appears strong when the retriever surfaces a small high-recall set, but weak when relevant evidence is missed or when too many chunks are passed to the generator.
mechanism: Generation quality depends non-monotonically on retrieved context size. If top-k is too small, key evidence is absent; if too large, the model is distracted by redundant/noisy chunks. A tuned retrieval stack in VideoRAG can outperform alternatives, while generic RAG with poor recall or oversized top-k can underperform full-context or non-RAG baselines.
predictions: Accuracy peaks at moderate top-k
Contradiction fades when recall@k is matched
minimal_test: Re-run the compared systems with a shared retriever and identical chunking, then sweep top-k (e.g., 1, 5, 10, 20, 100) while measuring retrieval recall@k and answer accuracy; if the papers disagree only because they operated at different recall/top-k points, their performance ordering should converge at the same recall-controlled setting.

hypothesis: The contradiction is moderated by evaluation protocol and prompt format: pairwise win-rate or subjective overall-score evaluations can favor retrieval-focused answers, while exact-match/benchmark scoring or full-context prompts can favor models that preserve broader discourse structure.

Different prompt templates can further bias whether the model synthesizes across chunks or overfits to retrieved snippets.
predictions: Win-rate gains exceed exact-match gains
Unified prompts reduce cross-paper disagreement
minimal_test: Take one shared set of long-context-QA examples and evaluate the same RAG and non-RAG outputs under both protocols: pairwise LLM-judge win rate and benchmark-style exact/graded accuracy, using identical answer prompts for all methods; if rankings swap across protocols or prompts, the contradiction is attributable to evaluation/prompt moderators.

hypothesis: The divergence is driven by context_length: RAG looks worse than full-context at short fixed budgets like 4k tokens but becomes favorable as the source context grows to extreme long-context regimes where selective retrieval reduces distraction.

predictions: At 4k tokens, full-context still beats RAG on answer quality.
On extreme long-context videos, RAG regains a positive margin over non-retrieval baselines.

mechanism: Large top_k injects redundant or weakly relevant chunks, increasing noise and attention competition inside the generator; a smaller top_k preserves retrieval precision and makes RAG beneficial, especially in long-context settings where overstuffing is tempting.
predictions: Top-5 or top-10 will score above top-100 on the same long-context-QA benchmark.
A weak or negative RAG result will become neutral or positive after reducing only top_k.
minimal_test: On BABILong or one benchmark used in arxiv:2502.01549v1, hold model, retriever, context_length, chunk size, and prompt fixed; sweep only top_k over 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 and measure answer accuracy or win rate relative to the same baseline.

The same model can show a positive RAG effect on video QA and a negative or null effect on text-only long-context QA.

scope_conditions.task: long-context-QA spanning video understanding and text benchmark QA

predictions: Cross-citations are rarer than shared forecasting terminology would predict
A shared ontology of actions-roles-skills aligns benchmark annotations with little remapping

scope_conditions.method: structured prediction with explicit entity-relation or role-skill outputs
scope_conditions.task: forecasting or reasoning over temporally evolving scenes

scope_conditions.task: evaluation transfer between QA-style reasoning and extraction-style forecasting

scope_conditions.method: models with explicit temporal state representations or extractable intermediate predictions
scope_conditions.task: future situation forecasting and situated reasoning

evidence_gap: No direct head-to-head import study currently shows whether structure-first modeling and decomposition-first evaluation transfer across the two communities.

hypothesis: The positive F1 result may reflect improved class-balanced veracity labeling, while the percent-based results may measure raw error incidence or failure-rate prevalence, so the same RAG outputs can look better on F1 yet still show high absolute harmful-output rates.
mechanism: F1 rewards the harmonic balance of precision and recall for discrete labels, especially under class imbalance, whereas percent metrics here appear to count occurrence rates of hallucination-like failures or unrecoverable cases without credit for improved ranking or thresholded decision quality.
predictions: High-F1 runs still show sizable raw failure percent.
Lower failure percent does not always raise label F1.
minimal_test: Collect one shared evaluation set with item-level RAG outputs and binary gold labels for hallucination/veracity. For each output, compute both: (a) veracity-label F1 after applying the model's existing decision rule, and (b) percent of outputs counted as hallucinated/leaking/unverifiable under the percent-style criterion. Cross-score the exact same outputs from the same model checkpoints and compare whether improvements in F1 can coexist with weak or adverse changes in percent.

scope_conditions.task: hallucination-mitigation when outputs are mapped to categorical veracity or hallucination labels

evidence_gap: The claims do not show whether the percent metrics were computed from the same label space or class distribution as the F1 evaluation.

hypothesis: The metrics may differ in whether they score correctness of the final judgment versus faithfulness/safety of the generated text, allowing RAG to improve F1 on verdict labels while still failing percent-based checks on leakage, unsupported details, or unverifiable content.
mechanism: A model can retrieve enough evidence to choose the right veracity class, boosting F1, yet still copy retrieved records, add unsupported rationales, or produce claims that cannot be grounded; percent metrics focused on such generation-level failures penalize those behaviors even when the final label is correct.
predictions: Correct veracity labels can still fail faithfulness checks.
Leakage-heavy outputs may preserve similar label F1.
minimal_test: On a shared set of prompts, save full RAG outputs including final label and explanation text. Then score the same outputs twice: once with label-level F1 against gold veracity labels, and once with a percent metric marking any output that contains unsupported, unverifiable, or near-verbatim retrieved content. Test whether many outputs counted correct by F1 are simultaneously counted as failures by the percent metric.

scope_conditions.task: hallucination-mitigation in settings that generate both a final answer and free-form rationale or retrieved-text-conditioned response

evidence_gap: The current claims do not report paired label-correctness and faithfulness annotations on identical outputs, so the extent of this dissociation is unknown.

hypothesis: The percent metrics may use a binary any-error definition while F1 aggregates instance-level decisions over all examples, so a small number of extra unsupported spans can sharply worsen percent without much affecting F1, creating apparent contradiction.
mechanism: If percent marks an example as failed when any hallucinated token/span, privacy leak, or unverifiable claim appears, it is highly sensitive to overgeneration and long responses; F1 on one final label per example is comparatively insensitive to those additional local errors as long as the top-level classification remains correct.
predictions: Longer answers worsen failure percent more than F1.
Trimming rationales improves percent with little F1 change.
minimal_test: Generate a paired prediction set by running the same RAG model on a shared benchmark twice: once with full unconstrained explanations and once with answer-only or length-capped outputs. Cross-score every output with both metrics: label F1 on the final decision and percent failed under an any-error rule for hallucinated/leaked/unverifiable spans. If percent changes much more than F1 across verbosity-controlled outputs, the definitional gap is implicated.

scope_conditions.task: hallucination-mitigation for factual generation or fact-checking where outputs may vary in length

evidence_gap: We lack output-length-controlled evaluations showing whether the reported percent outcomes are driven by any-error sensitivity rather than core decision quality.

hypothesis: Percent-based evaluations may be scoring response relevance or partial task completion, while accuracy-based evaluations require exact final answers, so prompting can look helpful on one metric and weak or harmful on the other for the same outputs.
mechanism: Prompting often adds intermediate reasoning and evidence usage that increases judged relevance, plausibility, or step coverage, but those same outputs can still miss the exact terminal answer; cross-scoring therefore yields higher percent scores than exact accuracy on identical generations.
predictions: Prompted outputs gain more on percent than on exact accuracy.
Many percent-positive cases remain accuracy-flat after answer extraction.
minimal_test: Take one shared multi-step-reasoning benchmark with gold final answers and a rubric for response relevance/step support. Generate one paired output set from the same models under prompted and baseline conditions, preserving raw completions. Then score every completion twice: (1) exact final-answer accuracy after deterministic answer extraction, and (2) percent relevance/partial-credit scoring via the benchmark rubric or blinded annotators. Compare per-item deltas between metrics for the same outputs.

scope_conditions.task: multi-step-reasoning

evidence_gap: The current claims do not show whether the reported Percent values were exact-match, relevance, or partial-credit measures, so metric semantics must be verified from the original evaluation protocols.

hypothesis: One metric may aggregate over all reasoning steps or sub-decisions, whereas the other collapses everything to a single end-to-end item outcome, causing prompting to appear beneficial when it improves intermediate steps but not full-chain success.
mechanism: In multi-step tasks, prompts can help local subproblem solving or evidence alignment without reliably propagating those gains through the whole chain; step-averaged percent therefore rises while item-level accuracy stays unchanged or drops if any single downstream error invalidates the answer.
predictions: Step-level percent improves more than item accuracy under prompting.
Accuracy drops concentrate on items with late-step error propagation.
minimal_test: Use a shared evaluation set with decomposable gold rationales or manually segmented solution steps. Collect one paired prediction set by running the same prompted model outputs once per item and saving full traces plus final answers. Score those exact outputs two ways: (1) step-level percent credit for each correctly completed substep or evidence alignment, and (2) binary item accuracy for the final answer only. Analyze whether prompt gains are larger at the step level than at the item level, especially on longer chains.

scope_conditions.task: multi-step-reasoning

evidence_gap: The claims do not report whether Percent was computed per item, per step, or per subtask, so the aggregation level behind each result remains unclear.

hypothesis: Accuracy may be computed after strict normalization of a final answer, while percent-based scoring may be more surface-form tolerant or judge-based, so prompting that changes verbosity and answer formatting can alter one metric much more than the other.
mechanism: Prompts often induce longer explanations, hedging, or multimodal descriptions; tolerant percent scoring can still mark such outputs as acceptable, but strict accuracy pipelines can fail due to extraction errors, non-canonical formatting, or multiple-answer emissions despite underlying correct reasoning.
predictions: Reformatting prompted outputs raises accuracy more than percent.
Verbose prompted answers have larger metric gaps than concise ones.
minimal_test: On a shared benchmark, generate a single set of raw prompted outputs and keep them unchanged. Cross-score each raw output with both metrics: (1) the original strict accuracy script using exact extraction/normalization, and (2) a tolerant percent evaluation such as rubric-based or semantic acceptability scoring. Then create a second view of the same outputs by applying deterministic post-processing to isolate the final answer span without changing content, and rescore with both metrics. If accuracy changes sharply while percent stays stable, the definitional gap is likely formatting sensitivity rather than reasoning quality.

scope_conditions.task: multi-step-reasoning

evidence_gap: We lack paired raw completions and official scoring scripts showing whether answer extraction or judge tolerance differed across the cited studies.

hypothesis: Terminology drift around evaluation causes psychology-style construct probing and EDA-style benchmark grading to be treated as different problems despite both targeting latent competence from text interactions.
mechanism: Psychology papers frame success as revealing behavioral strategies or constructs, while EDA papers frame success as tool-use accuracy and grade distributions, so keyword-based discovery and reviewer expectations fail to connect them.
predictions: EDA papers rarely use construct-validity language despite discussing comprehension
Psychology evaluation papers rarely cite tool-use benchmarks even when using text-based protocols
minimal_test: Run a focused literature comparison on 30 psychology evaluation papers and 30 EDA agent evaluation papers, coding terms for construct validity, comprehension, grading, and benchmark language; then re-evaluate a small EDA benchmark with deconfounded prompt pairs adapted from psycholinguistics to see whether grade changes expose heuristic reliance.

scope_conditions.task: text-based evaluation of behavioral competence or EDA tool interaction

evidence_gap: Direct evidence is missing on whether the same systems fail for analogous confounds when wording and surface cues are controlled.

hypothesis: Venue separation persists because psychology prioritizes explanation-oriented experiments while EDA prioritizes systems performance, but cross-pollination would be valuable if EDA imported deconfounded stimuli and psychology imported domain fine-tuning comparisons.
mechanism: Distinct methodological priors lead one community to optimize for interpretable contrasts and the other for leaderboard-like end performance, reducing mutual citation even when both study failures of general LLMs on text-mediated tasks.
predictions: Deconfounded EDA prompt variants will reduce apparent gains of general-purpose LLMs more than of fine-tuned models
Psychology-style tasks fine-tuned on narrow domains will show larger absolute accuracy gains but not always better construct sensitivity
minimal_test: Create matched EDA tasks with semantically similar but syntactically deconfounded prompt variants and compare GPT-4 versus AutoMage2-style fine-tuned models; separately fine-tune a small model on a psychology benchmark and test whether improved scores preserve psychometric contrasts such as priming or theory-of-mind manipulations.

scope_conditions.task: EDA tool-use evaluation and theory of mind or language-processing evaluation

evidence_gap: No small replication has yet shown whether domain fine-tuning and deconfounding affect the two communities' benchmarks in comparable ways.

hypothesis: A generational lag may persist because newer LLM results in both communities are moving quickly, but shared failure modes around text-only evaluation could be bridged by importing psychology's behavioral strategy analyses into EDA and EDA's rubric-based grading into psychology.
mechanism: High recent activity encourages each field to update against its own latest models and baselines rather than revisit older adjacent literatures, so parallel debates about what model behavior really means remain uncoupled.
predictions: Recent papers in both areas will cite newer model releases more often than adjacent-field evaluation methods
EDA rubric labels imported into psychology tasks will reveal separable partial-competence bands beyond binary accuracy
minimal_test: Compare citation half-life and method-citation patterns in recent papers from both communities, then run a small replication where psychology theory-of-mind responses are graded with an EDA-style multi-band rubric while EDA agent traces are annotated for behavioral strategies and latent construct errors.

scope_conditions.task: theory of mind tasks and EDA response quality evaluation

evidence_gap: We lack side-by-side annotation studies showing whether richer rubrics and strategy coding produce more comparable error taxonomies across the two communities.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because customer-service QA papers frame progress as benchmark gains under reference-based scoring, while psychology papers frame progress as construct-validity under behavioral probes, so each community may view the other's evaluation protocol as answering a different question.
mechanism: Terminology drift around 'evaluation' and methodological priors about what text outputs should measure can prevent citation even when both study text behavior under controlled prompts.
predictions: Customer-service papers rarely cite construct-validity or deconfounded-stimuli work
Psychology-style probes reveal failure modes not predicted by BLEU/ROUGE ranks
minimal_test: Compare citation vocabularies in 30 customer-service QA papers vs 30 psychology-inspired LLM evaluation papers, then run a small customer-service QA benchmark where top systems are additionally tested with deconfounded paraphrases and priming-based prompt variants to see whether leaderboard order changes.

scope_conditions.method: reference-based QA evaluation versus behavioral evaluation-methods
scope_conditions.task: text-based customer service question answering and psychology-style text evaluation

evidence_gap: Missing direct evidence on whether different evaluation language and validity criteria, rather than topic separation alone, explain the absent cross-citation.

hypothesis: A useful cross-pollination candidate is that psychology's deconfounded stimuli and priming controls could be imported into customer-service RAG evaluation, while customer-service knowledge-graph structure could be imported into psychology-style mental-state prompting to test whether explicit relational context changes apparent reasoning ability.
mechanism: Both communities may face hidden confounds from surface text cues: customer-service systems may exploit lexical overlap, and psychology benchmarks may overestimate reasoning when relational structure is implicit rather than explicitly manipulated.
predictions: KG-RAG gains shrink on deconfounded customer-service question variants
Explicit KG context improves consistency on theory-of-mind style text tasks
minimal_test: Take a small customer-service QA set and create deconfounded paraphrase pairs that preserve answerability while reducing lexical shortcuts; separately, convert a compact theory-of-mind benchmark into graph-annotated relational context and compare plain prompting against KG-augmented prompting using the same model.

scope_conditions.method: RAG with a knowledge graph plus psycholinguistic control techniques
scope_conditions.task: domain-QA and theory-of-mind/commonsense text reasoning

evidence_gap: No direct replication currently shows whether KG benefits survive deconfounding, or whether graph-structured context improves psychology-style benchmarks beyond prompt engineering.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist due to venue separation and generational lag: fast-moving customer-service RAG work cites recent engineering baselines and shared-model controls, whereas psychology-oriented LLM studies cite behavioral science lineages, delaying recognition that both are studying text responses under matched-model conditions.
mechanism: High recent activity can sustain parallel conversations when each venue rewards different baselines, review criteria, and bibliographies, even if both rely on the same underlying text generation models.
predictions: Cross-citations are lower across venue families than across task-neighbor papers within each family
Importing matched-model controls into psychology benchmarks changes fewer conclusions than importing behavioral controls into customer-service benchmarks
minimal_test: Build a focused bibliometric sample of recent customer-service RAG papers and psychology-inspired LLM evaluation papers, compare cross-venue citation rates and baseline sections, then replicate one psychology benchmark using the same-model/same-embedding control style from customer-service work and one customer-service benchmark using behavioral probe controls.

scope_conditions.method: same-model control comparisons and behavioral benchmark design
scope_conditions.task: text generation/evaluation across domain-QA and behavioral reasoning

evidence_gap: We lack a side-by-side literature map showing whether venue incentives and citation habits, rather than substantive incompatibility, account for the separation.

hypothesis: Accuracy is measuring final exact correctness on domain-QA items, while percent-style metrics are measuring intermediate revision or timing behavior, so the same outputs can look improved on process metrics without improving final answer correctness.
mechanism: A model can revise from false to true more often or answer earlier after key evidence appears, yet still end with nearly the same number of exact-correct final answers; process-sensitive percentages therefore rise while item-level accuracy stays flat.
predictions: Examples with high F2T gains show weak final-accuracy gains.
Early-first-answer cases increase percent metrics more than exact accuracy.
minimal_test: On a shared domain-QA set with turn-by-turn model outputs (e.g., MINT-style dialogues or newly generated multi-turn medical QA cases), keep the same outputs fixed and score each item twice: once with final-turn exact accuracy and once with process metrics matching the papers (F2T/T2F rates, percent first-answer-at-turn). Then compute whether systems or subsets that improve the percent metrics also show only small or inconsistent changes in final accuracy.

scope_conditions.task: domain-QA

evidence_gap: We still lack paired per-example outputs scored simultaneously by final accuracy and revision/timing percentages on the same cases.

graph_bridge.from: accuracy, percent, win rate, win rate; macro-average

hypothesis: Win rate is measuring pairwise preference or relative dominance against comparators, whereas accuracy/macro-average measure absolute task performance, so a model can win many head-to-head judgments without having the best absolute correctness profile.
mechanism: Pairwise win rate depends on the comparison pool, judge thresholds, and stylistic or partial-quality advantages; absolute metrics aggregate correctness per item or per subtask, which can flatten or reorder models even when one model wins more direct comparisons.
predictions: Higher win-rate models need not have higher exact accuracy.
Identical macro-averages can coexist with different pairwise win rates.
minimal_test: Take one shared domain-QA evaluation set and collect a single frozen set of model answers from the compared systems. Cross-score those same answers with both metrics: (1) exact accuracy or macro-average over task categories, and (2) pairwise win rate via blinded head-to-head judging on the same questions. Compare rank correlations and identify models whose pairwise win rate exceeds peers despite similar or lower absolute accuracy/macro-average.

scope_conditions.task: domain-QA

evidence_gap: The current claims do not provide a shared answer set with both pairwise judgments and absolute correctness labels for the same model outputs.

hypothesis: Macro-average is measuring balanced performance across subdomains, while raw accuracy or win rate are dominated by common or easier subsets, so the metrics diverge when model strengths are uneven across medical specialties or question types.
mechanism: A model that performs consistently across categories can tie on macro-average even if another model has higher overall wins or accuracy from excelling on high-frequency subsets; conversely, gains concentrated in one subset can raise aggregate metrics without changing balanced averages much.
predictions: Per-category reweighting changes raw rankings more than macro-average rankings.
Subset-skewed gains raise win rate/accuracy while leaving macro-average near-tied.
minimal_test: Using one shared domain-QA set with category labels (specialty, evidence type, or question type), obtain one fixed set of model predictions and score those same outputs three ways: raw accuracy, pairwise win rate, and macro-average across categories. Then resample or reweight category frequencies to test whether models with concentrated strengths move strongly on raw metrics but remain stable on macro-average.

scope_conditions.task: domain-QA

evidence_gap: We need category-labeled shared evaluations and per-category scores for the same predictions, not just headline metrics.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because moral-reasoning papers frame improvements as bias-reduction under prompting/RLHF while programming papers frame similar gains as functional correctness under pass@k or PAL, so shared reliability mechanisms are hidden by evaluation terminology drift.
mechanism: Different metric vocabularies and benchmark conventions partition attention: moral reasoning emphasizes discrimination/bias scores and ambiguous versus disambiguated contexts, while programming emphasizes pass@k, exact match, and execution success, preventing authors from recognizing that both study text-conditioned robustness and failure under alternative prompts.
predictions: Code-generation papers rarely cite BBQ-style ambiguous/disambiguated evaluation designs.
Importing ambiguous/disambiguated reporting into code benchmarks changes model rankings on some tasks.
minimal_test: Compare 30 moral-reasoning and 30 code-generation papers for metric terms and citation overlap, then run a small APPS or MBPP study where problem statements are rewritten to include ambiguous versus disambiguated natural-language context and report pass@k stratified by condition.

scope_conditions.task: safety and code-generation benchmarks with natural-language problem statements

evidence_gap: Direct evidence is missing on whether benchmark rewrites analogous to ambiguous/disambiguated contexts actually expose hidden reliability differences in code models.

hypothesis: Venue separation may sustain the disconnect, but cross-pollination would likely be productive: chain-of-thought plus instruction framing from moral reasoning could improve safety-sensitive code generation, while program-aided execution checks from programming could stabilize moral reasoning outputs.
mechanism: Safety papers often optimize verbal reasoning traces, whereas programming papers validate outputs through execution; each community therefore underuses the other's strongest intervention for controlling error propagation in text-to-decision tasks.
predictions: CoT-style instruction framing reduces unsafe or biased code comments/completions without hurting pass@k.
PAL-style external program checks improve consistency on moral reasoning judgments across prompt variants.
minimal_test: Run a 2x2 prompting study on a code benchmark with safety-laden prompts and on a moral reasoning benchmark: baseline versus Q+IF+CoT for code, and baseline versus program-aided structured verdict generation for moral scenarios, measuring pass@k plus toxicity/bias for code and judgment consistency/bias for moral tasks.

scope_conditions.task: reasoning-heavy code generation and moral judgment tasks that can be expressed with structured intermediate states

evidence_gap: There is little direct replication showing whether reasoning traces transfer benefits across domains when the end objective differs between executable correctness and normative judgment.

hypothesis: Methodological priors and generational lag may keep the communities apart: moral-reasoning researchers may view code models as narrow tools, while programming researchers may treat safety effects as peripheral, despite shared evidence that RLHF and model choice alter text behavior in nontrivial ways.
mechanism: Once a field settles on different default interventions—RLHF/prompt alignment for safety versus model-family and benchmark scaling comparisons for code—new papers inherit separate citation lineages, so active recent work continues parallel rather than integrated debates.
predictions: Papers on code models mention RLHF effects on social bias less often than similarly timed safety papers.
Applying RLHF-step comparisons to code benchmarks reveals non-monotonic tradeoffs between pass@k and toxicity/offensiveness.
minimal_test: Do a focused literature comparison of 2023 papers for mentions of RLHF, bias, pass@k, and toxicity across both communities, then replicate a small code-generation evaluation at multiple available alignment stages or instruction-tuned variants to test whether offensiveness and functional success move in different directions.

scope_conditions.task: code generation with auxiliary safety evaluation

evidence_gap: The missing evidence is a direct within-model study connecting alignment stage to both coding performance and social-safety metrics, analogous to the RLHF-step analysis in moral reasoning.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because business-agent papers frame text as a simulation environment while astronomy papers frame text as a retrieval substrate, so shared evaluation-protocol issues are not indexed as the same problem.
mechanism: Terminology drift plus venue separation can hide overlap: agentic-reasoning work emphasizes competition dynamics and simulated actors, whereas astronomy search work emphasizes HyDE, reranking, and top-k tuning for literature QA, leading citation search to miss common text-based evaluation concerns.
predictions: Business papers rarely mention HyDE or reranking despite text retrieval dependence
Astronomy papers rarely cite agent-simulation benchmarks when discussing evaluation protocol
minimal_test: Run a focused literature comparison on 20 business agent papers and 20 astronomy literature-search papers, coding title/abstract terminology and citation overlap; then import HyDE+rereanking from the astronomy setup into a text-grounded business competition benchmark and compare retrieval recall for strategy-relevant documents against TF-IDF.

scope_conditions.method: text-based agent simulation and RAG retrieval pipelines
scope_conditions.task: strategic reasoning in business competition vs literature search/question answering

evidence_gap: Missing are direct cross-domain ablations showing whether retrieval quality materially changes downstream business-agent behavior.

hypothesis: A methodological-priors split may sustain the disconnect: astronomy treats retrieval settings as first-class experimental variables, while business simulation treats the LLM world model itself as primary, so each community underestimates the other's strongest tool.
mechanism: When one field expects performance gains from retriever engineering and the other expects gains from richer agent interaction, negative transfer in framing discourages citation even when both rely on text selection and judgment.
predictions: Top-k sensitivity analyses will be common in astronomy and rare in business-agent studies
Adding agent-style multi-turn decision episodes to astronomy retrieval benchmarks will expose failure modes not seen in static QA metrics
minimal_test: Replicate a small slice of the astronomy benchmark with an agentic wrapper: after retrieving papers with semantic+HyDE+rereanking or TF-IDF, require a GPT-4 agent to make sequential accept/reject or planning decisions; compare whether retrieval gains persist under multi-turn reasoning. In the opposite direction, vary top-k and reranking in CompeteAI-style tasks and measure outcome stability.

scope_conditions.method: retrieval ablations and multi-turn LLM agents
scope_conditions.task: single/multi-paper factual retrieval and business competition episodes

evidence_gap: There is little direct evidence on whether retriever improvements transfer to sequential strategic tasks or whether agentic evaluation changes astronomy benchmark rankings.

hypothesis: Cross-pollination may be delayed by a generational lag in evaluation norms: astronomy has moved toward explicit LLM-as-judge agreement checks, while business-agent work still foregrounds illustrative simulation, so shared evaluation-protocol mechanisms remain uncited.
mechanism: Communities adopting newer evaluation conventions at different rates create asynchronous literatures; one side publishes benchmarked agreement statistics and retrieval recipes, while the other side publishes scenario demonstrations, delaying recognition of compatible tooling.
predictions: LLM-judge agreement reporting will appear more often in astronomy-adjacent evaluation papers than in business-agent papers
Importing GPT-4 accept/reject judging into business simulation outputs will reveal measurable disagreement with human raters on strategic quality
minimal_test: Sample outputs from a CompeteAI-style benchmark and score them with the accept/reject protocol used in the evaluation paper, then compare agreement with human business experts; conversely, apply business-style scenario-based evaluation to astronomy retrieval answers to test whether static accept/reject misses strategic or contextual errors.

scope_conditions.method: LLM-as-judge evaluation and human agreement analysis
scope_conditions.task: answer quality evaluation and simulated strategic reasoning

evidence_gap: We still lack paired human-and-LLM judgments on business-agent outputs and scenario-based audits on astronomy retrieval benchmarks.

hypothesis: A terminology and metric drift disconnect likely persists because finance frames shared problems as return/risk optimization while politics frames them as veracity/reliability assessment, obscuring that both are studying forecast reliability under evidence selection constraints.
mechanism: Different top-line metrics and task labels hide a common mechanism: retrieval/tool choices alter precision-recall balance and thus downstream reliability, but finance reports Sharpe/drawdown or tool TF1 while politics reports weighted F1 and veracity accuracy.
predictions: Shared error types appear after relabeling metrics into calibration and abstention terms
Papers in both communities rarely co-use keywords for precision-recall balance and reliability
minimal_test: Run a focused literature comparison on abstracts and evaluation sections from the cited finance and politics papers, mapping each metric to a common reliability taxonomy; then replicate RAGAR-style evidence retrieval on one finance QA/trading-explanation benchmark and compare calibration, abstention, and F1-style reliability metrics alongside native finance metrics.

scope_conditions.method: agent or RAG systems with explicit evidence retrieval/tool selection
scope_conditions.task: financial forecasting/QA and political claim verification where reliability is evaluated from retrieved evidence

evidence_gap: Missing are direct cross-community comparisons that normalize disparate metrics into a shared reliability framework.

hypothesis: Venue separation likely persists because finance prioritizes domain-specific benchmarks and data coverage while politics prioritizes fact-checking pipelines, so each community sees the other's datasets as out-of-domain rather than methodologically relevant.
mechanism: Benchmark construction choices create siloed citation networks: finance emphasizes specialized corpora and market/regulatory coverage, whereas politics emphasizes multimodal claim verification; this makes transfer look like dataset shift instead of a chance to test general evidence-grounding methods.
predictions: Benchmark papers cite same-domain datasets far more often than cross-domain evidence-grounding papers
Importing structural retrieval or RAGAR decomposition improves at least one out-of-domain benchmark over its native baseline
minimal_test: Construct a small citation comparison between benchmark-introduction papers in finance and political fact-checking papers, then run a replication importing finance structural-element chunking into a political claim-verification benchmark and importing RAGAR subquestioned retrieval into FinanceBench or IndiaFinBench.

scope_conditions.task: domain QA, regulatory QA, or claim verification with document evidence

evidence_gap: There is little direct evidence on whether the current benchmark designs suppress cross-domain transfer or whether methods fail only because of domain shift.

hypothesis: Cross-pollination has been slowed by methodological priors: finance often treats multimodal and tool-using agents as action systems, whereas politics treats retrieval and verification as conservative evidence aggregation, but each side has a technique the other could use immediately.
mechanism: Finance favors agentic execution and multimodal/tool interfaces, while politics favors decomposition plus retrieval-backed verification; these priors lead researchers to under-test the opposite toolkit even when failure modes like over-calling, unsupported evidence use, and modality mismatch are shared.
predictions: RAGAR-style CoVe verification reduces unsupported tool calls in financial agent benchmarks
Finance tool-selection precision analysis exposes over-calling failure modes in political multimodal verification
minimal_test: Two small replications: add CoVe/RAGAR-style self-verification to FinMCP-Bench or a trading-explanation agent and measure tool precision/EMR changes; add FinMCP-style tool precision, recall, and balanced multi-tool analysis to a political fact-checking pipeline with multimodal evidence and measure whether error diagnosis improves beyond weighted F1 alone.

scope_conditions.method: multimodal or tool-using LLM agents with intermediate actions
scope_conditions.task: financial tool use/trading support and political multimodal fact-checking

evidence_gap: Absent are direct ablation studies showing whether verification prompts from politics improve finance agents, and whether finance-style tool metrics reveal hidden failure modes in politics benchmarks.

hypothesis: The sign of tool-use on factual-QA flips with dataset composition: entity-heavy, open-domain questions benefit from retrieval tools, while datasets emphasizing verification or temporally constrained facts show weaker or mixed gains.
mechanism: When questions require missing world knowledge, Wikipedia search and QA tools add evidence that the base model lacks; when items are mainly verification-style or temporally narrow, tool calls add latency/noise and may not improve final answers enough to beat the initial output.
predictions: Gains are larger on open-domain entity questions than on verification subsets.
Matching question-type proportions across papers reduces the reported contradiction.
minimal_test: Construct a shared evaluation suite split into open-domain entity lookup, temporal fact recall, and factual-verification subsets; run the same tool-use pipeline and no-tool baseline on each subset with identical model, prompts, and decoding, then reweight each paper's reported aggregate to the same subset mixture to see whether the disagreement disappears.

scope_conditions.task: factual-QA including free-form, temporal, and verification-oriented questions

evidence_gap: The papers do not report sufficiently aligned per-question-type breakdowns to determine whether composition differences alone account for the sign change.

hypothesis: The contradiction is moderated by retrieval recall: tool-use helps only when the retriever consistently surfaces answer-bearing evidence, and looks ineffective or mixed when evidence recall is low.
mechanism: Interactive critique or answer revision can only improve outputs if the retrieved passages contain the needed fact; high-recall Wikipedia search enables corrective updates, whereas low-recall or poorly targeted retrieval yields irrelevant context that leaves the model unchanged or worsens answers.
predictions: Conditioning on answer-bearing retrieval makes tool-use gains positive in both setups.
Low-recall queries show small or negative delta versus the initial output.
minimal_test: For a common factual-QA benchmark, log whether the top-k retrieved results contain gold-supporting evidence, then compare tool-use minus no-tool performance separately on high-recall and low-recall items using the same retriever, k, and model; if the contradiction shrinks within matched-recall bins, recall is the moderator.

scope_conditions.method: tool-use relying on search/retrieval during answering or critique
scope_conditions.task: factual-QA with evidence retrievable from Wikipedia or similar corpora

evidence_gap: Neither side provides aligned retrieval-quality diagnostics such as answer-containing top-k rate, making it unclear whether one setup simply had better evidence access.

graph_bridge.from: Wikipedia search and question answering tools

hypothesis: The sign flips because prompt format and decoding strategy determine whether the model actually uses tool evidence rather than over-editing or ignoring it.
mechanism: Structured critique-and-revise prompts with controlled decoding can force evidence-grounded revisions, improving F1, whereas less constrained prompting or higher-variance decoding can cause unnecessary edits, hallucinated justifications, or failure to incorporate retrieved evidence, leading to mixed outcomes relative to the initial answer.
predictions: Evidence-grounded revision prompts outperform generic tool-access prompts on the same questions.
Greedy or low-temperature decoding reduces variance and narrows cross-paper differences.
minimal_test: Take one shared model and dataset, cross a generic tool-use prompt with a structured critique-and-revise prompt, and cross each with greedy versus sampled decoding while keeping tools and retrieval fixed; if the positive/mixed discrepancy vanishes under matched prompting and decoding, this moderator explains the contradiction.

scope_conditions.method: tool-use with answer revision or self-critique stages
scope_conditions.task: free-form factual-QA and factual verification

evidence_gap: The claims do not expose enough implementation detail about prompt templates, stopping rules, or decoding temperatures to assess whether formatting differences induced the divergent outcomes.

hypothesis: The apparent inconsistency arises because F1 rewards partial lexical overlap in free-form factual-QA answers, while accuracy/percent act closer to exact factual correctness or binary success, so tool-use can look helpful on one metric but not the other for the same outputs.
mechanism: Tool-use often adds supporting details, aliases, or longer answer strings; this can raise token overlap F1 even when the core fact is wrong or lower exact-match-style accuracy/percent when wording deviates from the reference.
predictions: Tool outputs beat baseline more on F1 than on accuracy on the same answers.
Longer tool-generated answers show larger F1 gains but smaller percent gains.
minimal_test: Take a shared factual-QA eval set with gold references that supports both short-answer exact scoring and free-form overlap scoring. Run the same tool-use system and its no-tool baseline once to produce one paired prediction file per model. Cross-score every identical prediction under both token-level F1 and exact/binary accuracy-or-percent after standard normalization, then compare per-example deltas and stratify by answer length.

scope_conditions.task: factual-QA with free-form or alias-rich answers

evidence_gap: The claims do not report whether the same outputs were ever rescored under both F1 and accuracy/percent, nor how answer normalization was handled.

hypothesis: The metrics diverge because percent in tool-use studies may measure tool-call usage or success frequency at the example level, whereas F1/accuracy measure final answer quality, so a method can score highly on percent-like operational metrics without improving answer correctness on the same cases.
mechanism: A tool can be invoked frequently and appear responsible for many examples, inflating percent-style summaries of usage or contribution, while the final textual answer receives little or no gain under correctness metrics.
predictions: Examples with successful tool invocation do not always show higher final-answer accuracy.
Percent contribution rankings of tools differ from rankings by F1 gain on the same items.
minimal_test: On a shared factual-QA benchmark, log the same model outputs together with tool traces for each example. Build a paired table containing final answer text, whether a tool was called, which tool was called, and whether the run is counted as tool-used under the study's percent definition. Then score the identical answers with F1 and accuracy, and separately compute percent-style tool-use rates/contribution rates on the same examples to test whether operational percent correlates weakly with answer-quality metrics.

scope_conditions.task: factual-QA where papers summarize tool usage in percent

evidence_gap: The payload does not specify whether 'Percent' in TempLAMA is answer accuracy percentage or a contribution/use-rate statistic, so the exact mapping from percent to quality is unresolved.

hypothesis: The conflict is driven by metric granularity: accuracy/percent collapse each item to all-or-nothing correctness, while F1 gives graded credit for partially correct multi-token answers, making tool-use seem more beneficial when it improves some facets of an answer without fixing the whole fact.
mechanism: Tool-use may retrieve evidence that corrects one entity, date component, or qualifier but leaves another component wrong; F1 rises because overlap improves, yet accuracy/percent stay flat because the entire answer is still judged incorrect.
predictions: Partially corrected answers gain F1 without changing binary accuracy.
Multi-entity or date-bearing questions show larger metric disagreement than single-span questions.
minimal_test: Create or sample a shared factual-QA set containing both single-span and compositional answers. Generate one fixed set of outputs from the same tool-use model and baseline. For those exact outputs, compute token F1 and binary accuracy/percent, then manually label a subset for partial-correction patterns such as one correct entity but wrong qualifier/date; test whether those items account for most cases where tool-use helps F1 but not accuracy.

scope_conditions.task: factual-QA with compositional, temporal, or multi-field answers

evidence_gap: Current claims report aggregate metric changes but not per-example error decomposition showing whether tool-use creates more partially correct answers.

hypothesis: Outcome metrics that require exact end-state correctness diverge from tool-call overlap metrics because they score different parts of the same agent trajectory.
mechanism: EMR/success/pass-style metrics collapse the whole trajectory into a strict all-or-nothing final outcome, while TF1/precision/recall/tool-precision reward partial correctness in tool selection even when execution or final answer fails; over-calling can improve recall-based scores while hurting exact success.
predictions: High-TF1 low-EMR cases will contain extra but plausible tool calls.
Rankings by EMR will penalize over-calling more than rankings by TF1.
minimal_test: On a shared tool-use eval set such as FinMCP-Bench or MCP-Bench, collect raw per-example outputs from the same agent runs: full tool-call traces and final answers. Cross-score each identical output with both exact outcome metrics (EMR, answer correctness, task success) and tool overlap metrics (TP/TR/TF1). Then inspect examples where TF1 is high but EMR/success is zero, and compare model rankings under both scorings.

scope_conditions.task: agentic-reasoning

evidence_gap: We still need paired per-example traces with both tool annotations and final-task labels, not just aggregate tables.

hypothesis: Efficiency-oriented metrics reward shorter cleaner trajectories, whereas success-oriented metrics reward task completion even if the path is redundant or costly.
mechanism: Metrics like CE, navigation steps, reversed redundancy ratio, and user-prompt count measure operational efficiency or frugality; success rate, completion ratio, and average return primarily value goal attainment. A model can take many unnecessary actions yet still finish, or be very concise yet fail the last subgoal.
predictions: Same-success trajectories will vary widely in CE or step count.
Models with top RRR/low steps will not always top success rate.
minimal_test: Using one shared web or mobile agent benchmark, run the same model outputs once and log complete trajectories. For each identical trajectory, compute both success-style metrics (success rate, completion ratio, average return) and efficiency-style metrics (CE, navigation steps, RRR, number of prompts). Cross-tab examples into success/efficient, success/inefficient, fail/efficient, fail/inefficient, and compare leaderboard reorderings under the two metric families.

scope_conditions.task: agentic-reasoning

evidence_gap: Current claims cite different summaries, but paired trajectory-level data scored by both efficiency and success metrics is missing.

hypothesis: Structural-validity metrics mainly assess format and action legality, while task-level metrics assess semantic accomplishment, creating apparent inconsistency when agents follow schemas correctly but do not solve the task.
mechanism: Schema compliance, valid tool naming, reasonable operation ratio, and subgoal legality can be high because the agent emits well-formed, allowable actions; however, task success, payoff, or completion can remain low if the chosen legal actions are strategically wrong or incomplete. This definitional gap is especially salient in currently active agent benchmarking disagreements.
predictions: Many failed tasks will still have high schema compliance or ROR.
Ranking by validity metrics will correlate weakly with final payoff or task success.
minimal_test: On a shared eval set with executable trajectories, save the same agent outputs including raw action strings, tool names, and final states. Cross-score each output with validity metrics (schema compliance, valid tool naming, reasonable operation ratio) and outcome metrics (task success rate, payoff, completion). Measure correlation and inspect examples that are fully valid but unsuccessful versus successful despite minor format defects.

scope_conditions.task: agentic-reasoning

evidence_gap: Need per-example annotations linking action legality/format validity to downstream task outcomes on the same executions.

graph_bridge.from: schema compliance and valid tool naming

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because the two communities describe the same bottleneck with different task vocabularies: video papers frame it as long-context or spatio-temporal modality alignment, while biology papers frame it as hybrid DNA-text reasoning or trait/species QA, obscuring shared multimodal retrieval and fusion mechanisms.
mechanism: Terminology drift plus venue-specific benchmarking hides that both sides study how text-conditioned evidence selection and cross-modal fusion behave under weak supervision and long dependency chains.
predictions: Cross-citation rates stay low even when abstracts share multimodal and text terms
A concept mapping between modality alignment and hybrid fusion yields many method-level matches
minimal_test: Run a focused literature comparison on 30 video-understanding and 30 biology multimodal papers, normalize terms for retrieval, fusion, alignment, and reasoning, then measure how many uncited method pairs become nearest neighbors in embedding space after term normalization.

scope_conditions.method: multimodal or hybrid models with text-conditioned inference
scope_conditions.task: long-context video QA, biological reasoning, or biology image QA

evidence_gap: We lack a controlled audit showing whether citation failure disappears after terminology normalization rather than reflecting truly different technical problems.

hypothesis: Cross-pollination would likely be most productive if biology benchmarks imported video-style retrieval-augmented and agentic evidence selection, while video benchmarks imported biology-style reinforcement refinement for multimodal hybrids; the disconnect persists because methodological priors differ on whether to solve hard reasoning with external tools or end-to-end model adaptation.
mechanism: Video understanding currently rewards explicit search over long sequences, whereas biology work in this payload emphasizes stronger backbone scaling and GRPO-based refinement, so each community underexplores the other's optimization axis.
predictions: Agentic retrieval improves biological reasoning accuracy on evidence-heavy questions
GRPO improves long-context video QA more for larger multimodal backbones than smaller ones
minimal_test: Small replication study: add a VideoRAG or DVD-style retrieval-and-tool pipeline to a KEGG-derived biological reasoning benchmark, and separately apply GRPO to a strong video QA multimodal baseline on a long-context benchmark, comparing gains by question length and backbone size.

scope_conditions.method: RAG or agentic search on one side; PPO-or-GRPO on the other
scope_conditions.task: evidence-heavy biological reasoning and long-context video question answering

evidence_gap: No head-to-head study currently tests external retrieval versus RL refinement across both domains under matched budgets and model sizes.

hypothesis: The disconnect may also persist because evaluation cultures are separated: video work uses fine-grained temporal/spatial and long-sequence metrics, while biology often reports end-task accuracy or F1, so apparent successes and failures are not directly comparable even when both involve text-grounded multimodal reasoning.
mechanism: Venue separation and incompatible evaluation protocols make it hard to see shared failure modes such as spatial-vs-temporal tradeoffs, trait-vs-species decomposition, or gains that vanish under localization-sensitive metrics.
predictions: Biology VLM rankings change when localization-style submetrics are added
Video model rankings change when coarse trait-style text QA replaces localization-heavy scoring
minimal_test: Construct a small shared benchmark slice with paired coarse and fine-grained labels: for biology images add region/part grounding metrics analogous to AP@vIoU or J&F, and for video add coarse attribute/species-style text QA; then compare whether model rankings and claimed strengths remain stable.

scope_conditions.task: spatial grounding, reasoning segmentation, trait identification, and species classification

evidence_gap: We do not yet know whether the communities disagree about models or simply about what counts as success because the same systems have not been scored under imported metrics.

hypothesis: Terminology drift around safety causes autonomous driving work on reliability risk to frame failures as planning uncertainty while human-agent interaction frames similar failures as social influence or bias, reducing cross-citation despite overlapping text-mediated risk mechanisms.
mechanism: Different labels and evaluation vocabularies partition similar phenomena: driving papers emphasize uncertainty, trajectories, and open-loop planning, whereas HAI papers emphasize trust, bias, and influence, so search and reviewer matching fail even when both study text-conditioned unsafe behavior.
predictions: Keyword overlap is low despite shared references to safety risk and text
Imported HAI risk annotations reveal unsafe persuasion-style rationales in driving planner outputs
minimal_test: Compare abstracts and evaluation sections from a small corpus in both communities using embedding-based topic alignment versus keyword overlap; then annotate a sample of LLM-based driving planner explanations with an HAI-inspired taxonomy for influence, overconfidence, and bias to see whether non-driving social-risk labels capture failure cases missed by standard driving metrics.

scope_conditions.method: text-mediated agent reasoning and LLM-based planning
scope_conditions.task: autonomous driving planning and human-agent interaction safety analysis

evidence_gap: Missing direct evidence that the same output patterns are being differently named rather than genuinely different behaviors.

hypothesis: Venue separation and methodological priors persist because autonomous driving prioritizes benchmarked 3D/task performance while human-agent interaction prioritizes context-sensitive behavioral validity, so each community discounts the other's evidence as weak on its preferred axis.
mechanism: Driving papers reward measurable gains on datasets like DriveLM Q&A and nuScenes, while HAI papers foreground subtle, context-dependent social effects and limitations; this creates asymmetric incentives against citing work that lacks the locally valued metrics or ecological framing.
predictions: Driving papers cite benchmark improvements more often than external validity caveats
HAI papers rarely use imported driving-style held-out benchmarks for social-risk claims
minimal_test: Run a focused literature comparison on 20 papers per community coding venue type, metric style, and presence of caveat language; then replicate one HAI text-interaction study with a fixed benchmark split and quantitative error taxonomy borrowed from autonomous driving evaluation to test whether the result becomes more portable across contexts.

scope_conditions.method: benchmark-driven multimodal evaluation versus human-subject interaction studies
scope_conditions.task: Q&A/open-loop planning and social identity effects in HAI

evidence_gap: No direct side-by-side audit yet shows that reviewer or venue norms, rather than topic mismatch, are the main barrier.

hypothesis: Cross-pollination is most likely through counterfactual data generation from autonomous driving into HAI and through HAI-style bias/influence stress tests into driving, because both communities study scaling behavior of text-conditioned agents under rare but safety-critical edge cases.
mechanism: Counterfactual annotation can systematically vary social or situational cues in HAI to create denser supervision, while HAI stress-testing can expose whether driving language planners produce unsafe or biased decisions under identity- or framing-laden prompts that standard planning metrics miss.
predictions: Counterfactual augmentation improves HAI detection of subtle risk effects with fewer subjects
HAI-style bias prompts change LLM driving planner outputs without large changes in standard trajectory scores
minimal_test: Create a small HAI benchmark with counterfactual rewrites of agent identity, group membership, and wording modeled on OmniDrive-style annotation, and measure label consistency; separately, apply identity- and framing-based prompt perturbations from HAI to an LLM driving planner and compare changes in chosen plans, rationales, collision/TTC metrics, and qualitative safety violations.

scope_conditions.method: counterfactual reasoning plus prompt-based stress testing
scope_conditions.task: text-based social interaction judgments and LLM-assisted autonomous driving planning

evidence_gap: It remains untested whether gains from counterfactual supervision transfer outside 3D driving, and whether HAI perturbations produce practically meaningful planning failures.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because hallucination-mitigation papers frame the core problem as factual grounding while web-agent papers frame it as action grounding, even though both report unsupported intermediate assertions as a reliability risk.
mechanism: Terminology drift splits adjacent failure modes into 'hallucinations' versus 'planning/grounding errors', reducing keyword overlap and cross-citation despite shared evaluation concerns.
predictions: Keyword co-occurrence between 'hallucination' and 'web agent' is sparse despite shared mention of grounding/evaluation.
Adding citation-enforced source traces to a web-agent benchmark lowers unsupported-action errors on text-heavy sites.
minimal_test: Run a focused bibliometric comparison of titles/abstracts for grounding-related terms across the two literatures, then replicate a web-agent baseline with a Citation-Enhanced Generation or explicit-source-citation prompt on a subset of WebArena/WorkArena tasks that require reading factual page content.

scope_conditions.method: citation-enhanced generation or source-citing RAG prompts
scope_conditions.task: text-heavy web tasks with verifiable page evidence rather than pure GUI manipulation

evidence_gap: Missing are direct ablations that classify web-agent failures into unsupported factual assertions versus interface-execution failures using the same annotation scheme as hallucination studies.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because the communities are separated by evaluation priors: hallucination work emphasizes segment- or claim-level factual metrics, while web work emphasizes end-to-end task success, so each side underestimates the other's tooling.
mechanism: Venue and methodological separation makes fine-grained factuality tools look irrelevant to agent benchmarks, and end-to-end browser metrics look too coarse for mitigation papers, even when both study safety-critical reliability.
predictions: Web-agent papers use end-to-end success more often than claim-level factual metrics, while hallucination papers show the reverse.
Importing factual-segment AUC-PR style evaluation into web traces reveals error differences not visible in task success alone.
minimal_test: Compare metric distributions in 20 papers from each community, then annotate a small shared sample of web-agent trajectories with factual/nonfactual segment labels and compute whether those labels predict eventual task failure beyond existing success metrics.

scope_conditions.method: segment-level factuality annotation and judge-model scoring
scope_conditions.task: web episodes with substantial natural-language reasoning or form-filling explanations

evidence_gap: There is little direct evidence that claim-level factuality metrics correlate with online task completion or safety incidents in web benchmarks.

hypothesis: Cross-pollination may be limited by a generational lag in which newer web-agent work has not yet absorbed retrieval-grounding and self-correction techniques from hallucination mitigation, while hallucination work has not adopted multimodal online environments as stress tests for its methods.
mechanism: Rapid recent activity produces parallel toolchains: hallucination papers refine RAG/self-correction for text, and web papers race toward multimodal action performance, leaving little time for mutual replication despite balanced impact.
predictions: Web-agent benchmarks rarely test retrieval-backed self-correction loops before acting, even on knowledge-intensive tasks.
Applying multimodal grounding from web agents to hallucination benchmarks improves performance more on text+image settings than text-only settings.
minimal_test: Small replication study in two directions: add retrieval-backed self-correction before action execution on a knowledge-intensive web subset, and add choice-based multimodal grounding from GPT-4V-style web agents to a text+image hallucination benchmark such as HallusionBench; compare against the original baselines.

scope_conditions.method: RAG self-correction for community_to; multimodal choice-based grounding for community_from
scope_conditions.task: knowledge-intensive web tasks and text+image hallucination evaluation

evidence_gap: What is missing are paired transfer experiments showing whether gains come from the shared mechanism of grounding or from model-scale differences alone.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because the two communities describe the same bottleneck with different task language—'action sequence prediction' versus 'document understanding'—so shared multimodal text-alignment advances are not retrieved as relevant prior work.
mechanism: Terminology drift hides a common mechanism: both settings require grounding text tokens to visually structured regions, but one community frames errors as agent planning failures while the other frames them as layout/reading failures.
predictions: Cross-community keyword overlap is low despite shared citations to multimodal LLM papers
A layout-aware encoder from document understanding improves desktop/web action score over text-only prompting
minimal_test: Run a focused literature comparison on titles/abstracts/keywords for the two paper sets, then replicate a desktop/web benchmark with a DocLLM-style layout-aware input representation replacing plain OCR/text prompts and measure action and sequence scores.

scope_conditions.method: multimodal models with explicit text-region alignment versus prompt-only text input
scope_conditions.task: desktop/web agent tasks that expose screenshots or UI text

evidence_gap: Direct ablations showing whether gains come from layout-text alignment rather than generic model scale or visual capacity are still missing.

hypothesis: Venue and benchmark separation may sustain the disconnect: desktop/web papers optimize executable action metrics, while document understanding papers optimize VQA/KIE/CLS metrics, so each side discounts the other's methods as misaligned with its evaluation culture.
mechanism: Methodological priors are benchmark-driven; communities preferentially adopt tools that already report gains on their native metrics, even when the underlying failure mode is shared visual-text reasoning.
predictions: Document-understanding papers rarely report executable decision metrics even when tasks require multi-step reasoning
Importing agent-style chain-of-actions prompting into document VQA helps more on reasoning-heavy questions than on KIE/CLS
minimal_test: Compare evaluation sections across the two literatures to quantify metric mismatch, then run a small replication on a document VQA benchmark using desktop/web-style stepwise action decomposition or script-like intermediate plans before answering.

scope_conditions.method: stepwise prompting or action-plan decomposition transferred across benchmarks
scope_conditions.task: reasoning-heavy document VQA rather than extraction-heavy KIE/CLS

evidence_gap: There is little direct evidence on whether metric mismatch alone blocks citation, versus deeper architectural incompatibilities.

hypothesis: Cross-pollination may be delayed by a generational lag in model assumptions: document understanding has moved toward explicit layout-sensitive multimodality, while desktop/web work still often contrasts prompt-only or code-oriented baselines, and each side underestimates the other's strongest transferable tool.
mechanism: Communities inherit different strong baselines—code-generation priors for agents and layout-aware multimodality for documents—so citation lags until a transfer experiment demonstrates that each prior helps on the other's benchmark.
predictions: Code-oriented models help document tasks with form-like procedural dependencies more than free-form VQA
Layout-aware document representations help desktop/web tasks with dense on-screen text more than icon-dominant tasks
minimal_test: Perform two small transfer studies: evaluate a code-generation-trained model on document benchmarks split by procedural versus non-procedural questions, and evaluate a DocLLM-style representation on desktop/web subsets stratified by text density.

scope_conditions.method: code-generation priors and layout-aware multimodal encoders
scope_conditions.task: text-dense interfaces and documents with procedural or structured dependencies

evidence_gap: No head-to-head study yet isolates when code priors versus layout priors dominate after controlling for model size and vision access.

hypothesis: The contradiction is partly driven by dataset composition: agent gains are positive on tasks dominated by decomposable, tool-grounded subgoals but negative or muted on composite tasks with fragile long-horizon dependencies.
mechanism: When benchmarks contain many tool-grounded or modular subtasks, agents can externalize reasoning into retrieval, planning, or tool calls and accumulate wins; when the task mix shifts toward tightly coupled composite objectives, multi-step coordination errors compound and any agent overhead can outweigh benefits, flipping the average effect sign.
predictions: Agent advantage is positive on modular/tool-rich subsets
Agent advantage shrinks or reverses on composite long-horizon subsets
minimal_test: Construct a shared evaluation slice across reported benchmarks by annotating tasks for compositionality/modularity (single-step or decomposable vs composite long-horizon). Recompute agent-minus-baseline effects within each stratum using the same model backbone and prompt budget; if the contradiction is due to composition, effect signs should align within strata and the cross-paper disagreement should largely disappear after matching task mix.

scope_conditions.task: agentic-reasoning

evidence_gap: The current claims rarely provide a common compositionality taxonomy or matched subset results across papers.

hypothesis: The disagreement is moderated by prompt/interface format: agents look strong when action spaces are exposed in structured or multimodal formats, but weaker when the same tasks are presented through lossy text/HTML or differently serialized tool schemas.
mechanism: Structured XML, schema-constrained tool calls, screenshots, or multimodal grounding reduce action ambiguity and hallucinated operations, whereas text-only or HTML-heavy interfaces force latent parsing and increase planning noise; this can reverse whether an agentic wrapper helps or hurts.
predictions: Structured/XML/schema prompts improve agent success relative to text-only
Format-matched evaluations reduce cross-paper sign disagreement
minimal_test: Take one shared family of tasks and run the same backbone agent under two strictly controlled interface conditions: structured/XML/schema-constrained or multimodal input versus text/HTML serialization, with identical decoding and budgets. Then compare agent-minus-baseline effects within each format. If the contradiction is format-driven, papers using the same interface should converge in sign.

scope_conditions.task: agentic-reasoning

evidence_gap: Most claims compare different interfaces and benchmarks simultaneously, so format is not isolated from task difficulty.

hypothesis: Model scale/backbone quality is the moderator: agentic scaffolding helps stronger backbones that can exploit planning and memory, but hurts or fails to help smaller or weaker reasoning models because the scaffold amplifies error propagation and tool overcalling.
mechanism: Large backbones can use intermediate plans, memory retrieval, and tool schemas effectively, converting extra structure into higher recall or better navigation; smaller models or weaker reasoning backbones incur more hallucinations, redundant actions, and poor tool selection, so the same agent design can produce a negative net effect.
predictions: Agent gains increase with stronger backbones
Small or weaker reasoning models show flat or negative agent gains
minimal_test: Evaluate the same agent scaffold and the same non-agent baseline across a ladder of backbones (small, medium, large; same provider where possible) under identical prompts, retrieval, and decoding. Plot agent-minus-baseline effect by model scale. If scale is the hidden moderator, the contradiction should collapse into a monotonic or thresholded pattern, with sign changes occurring at specific backbone strengths.

scope_conditions.task: agentic-reasoning

evidence_gap: Existing claims mix architecture changes with backbone substitutions, so it is unclear whether observed wins/losses come from the agent scaffold or the underlying model quality.

hypothesis: The divergent agent results are primarily driven by context_length, with longer windows helping multi-step agentic tasks until excessive context introduces distraction or hallucination.
mechanism: Agentic-reasoning often requires carrying plans, tool traces, observations, and intermediate state across many steps; increasing context_length can preserve needed state and improve recall on composite tasks, but overly long windows can also dilute salience and increase off-target reasoning, producing mixed effects across papers.
predictions: On WebArena, success rate rises from short to medium context before plateauing or slightly dropping at the longest setting.
On composite tool-use benchmarks, short-context runs show more truncation or lost-state errors than medium-context runs.
minimal_test: On WebArena, keep the same agent, backbone model, prompts, tools, and evaluation protocol fixed; sweep context_length only (e.g., 4k, 8k, 16k, 32k, 64k) and measure task success, step count, and truncation/lost-state errors.

scope_conditions.task: agentic-reasoning

evidence_gap: The current record contains many positive and mixed claims but few controlled ablations where only context_length changes for the same agent on the same benchmark.

hypothesis: The responsible setting variable is context_length because benchmark/task difficulty mediates how much history the agent must retain, causing the same agent to look strong on short-horizon tasks and weak on long-horizon ones when the window is constrained.
mechanism: When task instances require more accumulated observations, tool outputs, or subgoal memory, a fixed short context window forces compression or omission of earlier state; this disproportionately hurts composite and multi-turn variants while leaving simpler tasks relatively intact, yielding apparent contradictions across papers.
predictions: On MCP-Bench multi-server tasks, increasing context_length improves multi-server scores more than single-server scores.
On FinMCP-Bench, harder or multi-tool subsets benefit more from extra context than easy single-tool subsets.
minimal_test: On FinMCP-Bench and/or MCP-Bench, hold agent architecture, model, tool interface, and prompts fixed; sweep only context_length across several values and report scores separately for easy vs hard, single-tool vs multi-tool, and single-server vs multi-server subsets.

scope_conditions.task: agentic-reasoning

evidence_gap: Existing claims suggest interaction with task complexity, but there is no benchmark-normalized sweep isolating context_length across difficulty strata for the same system.

hypothesis: Context_length is the likely flip variable because some reported gains from memory, planning, or multi-agent structure may disappear once the effective context budget is equalized, meaning papers with different windows are comparing different state capacities rather than the same agent behavior.
