mechanism: Extra modules such as memory retrieval or planning traces often help by expanding effective working memory; if one paper grants a larger context_length, the base agent can already retain enough state, shrinking the benefit, whereas under tighter windows the same augmentation appears strongly positive.
predictions: The advantage of memory-augmented agents over memory-free agents is larger at 4k or 8k context than at 32k or 64k.
Single-agent versus structured-agent gaps shrink when context_length is increased while all prompts and tools stay fixed.
minimal_test: On WebArena, evaluate the same backbone under two fixed agent variants already present in the literature (memory-free vs memory-augmented, or single-agent vs structured-agent) while sweeping only context_length; compare how the performance delta between variants changes across 4k, 8k, 16k, 32k, and 64k.

scope_conditions.task: agentic-reasoning

evidence_gap: The literature reports gains from memory and structure, but rarely tests whether those gains persist after matching context_length across variants.

hypothesis: The reported benefit of agent methods flips with dataset composition: gains appear on decomposable symbolic/structured reasoning benchmarks but shrink or reverse on noisier open-ended or adversarially misleading reasoning sets.
mechanism: Agent interaction helps when tasks can be partitioned into checkable intermediate steps, but on ambiguous or misinformation-heavy items agents reinforce plausible wrong paths and add error propagation.
predictions: Agent gains are larger on math/chess/temporal subsets.
Agent gains drop on misleading or open-ended subsets.
minimal_test: Evaluate the same agent implementation and single-model baseline on a stratified benchmark split by problem type (symbolic math, temporal reasoning, open-ended research, misinformation/noise). Match model, prompt, budget, and decoding across splits; test whether contradictions across papers disappear after conditioning on dataset subtype.

scope_conditions.task: multi-step-reasoning

evidence_gap: Most claims omit dataset-level breakdowns, especially error rates by reasoning subtype and noise level.

hypothesis: The contradiction is moderated by model scale/base capability: stronger reasoning models benefit less or only on harder levels, while weaker models show larger improvements or collaboration-specific failure modes.
mechanism: Agents provide externalized search and self-correction that compensate for weaker base reasoning, but high-capability models already internalize much of this process, so extra agents add redundancy, latency, or correlated errors; very weak models may also amplify mistakes under collaboration.
predictions: Smaller/weaker models show larger average agent uplift.
Top-tier models show gains only on hardest levels.
minimal_test: Run one fixed agent scaffold over a capability ladder of base models (e.g., GPT-3.5-class, GPT-4-class, QwQ, DeepSeek-R1-class) on the same benchmark with identical prompts, tool access, and compute budget; compare agent-minus-single-model deltas by model strength and difficulty level to see whether the conflicting papers align once scale is fixed.

scope_conditions.task: multi-step-reasoning

evidence_gap: Current claims mix different base models and rarely report interaction plots between model capability and agent benefit.

hypothesis: The sign of the effect is moderated by prompting/decoding protocol, especially debate length and whether agents are encouraged to maintain disagreement versus converge quickly.
mechanism: Agent systems need enough independent reasoning diversity and controlled reconciliation; short or convergence-biased prompts collapse to one trajectory, while overlong or stubborn debate can entrench wrong answers, making accuracy improvements highly protocol-sensitive.
predictions: Intermediate debate rounds outperform very short or very long runs.
Prompts enforcing diversity beat early-consensus prompts.
minimal_test: Reproduce the same benchmark with a factorial design over prompt protocol: number of rounds, stance-persistence instruction strength, critic-actor vs majority voting, and deterministic vs sampled decoding. Hold dataset and base model fixed; test whether papers' opposing outcomes converge when using the same protocol.

scope_conditions.task: multi-step-reasoning

evidence_gap: Many papers describe agent prompts only partially and do not standardize decoding temperature, round count, or consensus rules.

hypothesis: Terminology drift around multi-hop structure versus task exposure may hide a shared mechanism in which graph-based aggregation over textual task descriptions improves reliability estimates for occupation-level risk.
mechanism: Knowledge-graph work frames the problem as retrieval for faithful reasoning, while labor economics frames it as exposure annotation and aggregation; the same latent need to combine scattered text evidence across linked entities is described with different vocabularies, so citation search misses the overlap.
predictions: KG papers rarely mention occupation exposure or task aggregation
Graph aggregation over task texts reduces annotation variance versus flat prompting
minimal_test: Compare keyword and citation neighborhoods for papers on graph retrieval and occupation exposure, then run a small replication on O*NET-style task texts where tasks and occupations are linked in a bipartite graph and evaluate whether a simple GNN-RAG style retriever improves agreement with human exposure labels over direct GPT-4 classification.

scope_conditions.method: graph neural retrieval or neighborhood aggregation over linked text units
scope_conditions.task: occupation-level task exposure assessment from textual task descriptions

evidence_gap: There is little direct evidence that graph-structured retrieval has been tried on labor exposure benchmarks with human-labeled reliability outcomes.

hypothesis: Venue separation persists because the two communities optimize different reliability criteria, but cross-pollination would be productive if labor economics imported faithfulness-style retrieval controls from KG QA and KG researchers imported human-adjudicated reliability auditing from labor annotation studies.
mechanism: KG papers are rewarded for Hit/F1 on benchmark QA, whereas labor economics emphasizes externally interpretable estimates and human comparison; this creates methodological priors that discourage borrowing even when both communities study failure under incomplete textual evidence.
predictions: KG benchmarks underreport human agreement or calibration diagnostics
Labor exposure studies do not compare structured retrieval against answer-only prompting
minimal_test: Perform a focused literature comparison of evaluation sections in recent KG-RAG and labor-exposure papers, then import KG-style retrieved-evidence constraints into an occupation exposure annotation pipeline and measure changes in human-GPT agreement and justification consistency.

scope_conditions.method: retrieval-augmented prompting with explicit evidence traces plus human audit
scope_conditions.task: text-based classification or QA where outputs must be justified from retrieved context

evidence_gap: Missing are side-by-side studies that evaluate the same text classification task under both benchmark-centric and human-audit-centric protocols.

hypothesis: Generational lag may sustain the disconnect: newer LLM-plus-graph methods are moving quickly, while labor economics still relies on prompt-based annotation pipelines, so a concrete bridge would be to test whether occupation-task relations can be treated as a lightweight knowledge graph and whether labor-style subgroup analysis can reveal hidden failure modes in KG QA.
mechanism: High recent activity with balanced impact suggests active development rather than a settled dispute; newer graph-RAG methods may not yet have diffused into economics, and economics-style stratification by job zone or wage group has not been adopted in KG QA for fairness or robustness slices.
predictions: Occupation-task graphs yield larger gains on multi-step exposure judgments than single-step ones
Subgroup slicing of KG QA reveals larger retrieval gains on harder question strata
minimal_test: Build a small occupation-task graph from existing task descriptions and compare flat LLM prompting against graph-based retrieval on cases requiring multiple linked task inferences; conversely, re-evaluate a KG QA benchmark using labor-style stratified reporting by question complexity and entity-frequency subgroup to test whether gains concentrate in specific strata.

scope_conditions.method: lightweight graph construction plus stratified evaluation
scope_conditions.task: multi-step text reasoning over linked entities or tasks

evidence_gap: No direct replication appears to test graph-RAG on labor datasets or subgroup-style robustness reporting on KG retrieval benchmarks.

hypothesis: The percent metric is measuring success rate on judged outputs, while cost per bug measures resource efficiency per resolved instance, so the same agent outputs can look strong on outcome quality yet weak on efficiency.
mechanism: If an agent uses many interaction rounds to secure a subset of successful repairs, win-rate or plausible-patch percent can rise even as tokens consumed per resolved bug remain high; the definitional gap is success-only numerator versus cost-normalized denominator.
predictions: High-percent successes cluster in the highest-token trajectories.
Re-scoring by success-per-1k-tokens narrows or reverses the percent advantage.
minimal_test: Collect a shared set of agent runs on the same code-reasoning benchmark with full transcripts, token counts, and final patches. For each run, compute both (a) the original percent-style success label used in prior work (judge win or plausible/fixed outcome) and (b) cost-per-bug / success-per-token from the same transcripts. Cross-score identical outputs by plotting success labels against token cost and recomputing rankings under both metrics.

scope_conditions.task: code-reasoning

evidence_gap: The current claims do not report paired per-instance token usage and success labels on the same examples, so efficiency-quality tradeoffs cannot yet be quantified directly.

hypothesis: The two percent-style results are themselves measuring different things: one rewards surface-level judged superiority of final code outputs, while the other measures executable repair hit rate, causing divergence when outputs look good to a judge but do not reliably fix bugs.
mechanism: A judge-based percent can credit readability, plausibility, or comparative preference in final answers, whereas plausible-patch/fixed-bug percent is tied to repair validity under tests; the definitional gap is subjective comparative evaluation versus operational bug-fix success.
predictions: Some judge-winning patches fail repair tests on the same bugs.
Test-passing rate correlates less with judge percent than with plausible-patch percent.
minimal_test: Assemble a shared eval set of code-reasoning problems where the agent produces final code outputs or patches. For the exact same outputs, obtain both a judge-based percent score (e.g., GPT-4 or human pairwise preference) and a repair-grounded score (plausible patch, fixed under tests). Compare instance-level agreement, especially among cases that win under judgment but fail execution-based validation.

scope_conditions.task: code-reasoning

evidence_gap: The cited claims do not provide the same outputs scored by both a judge-based metric and a repair-validity metric, so it is unclear whether apparent quality translates into actual bug resolution.

hypothesis: The metrics differ in conditioning: one percent is computed over all compared tasks, while cost per bug and plausible-patch percent are effectively conditioned on bug-fixing attempts and bug status, so aggregation over different denominators can make the agent appear inconsistent.
mechanism: A broad percent win rate can average across easier or non-repair code tasks, but cost per bug and 7% vs 44% focus on bug-repair episodes or subsets like fixed versus unfixed bugs; the definitional gap is unconditional aggregate performance versus conditional within-bug or within-attempt performance.
predictions: Percent drops when restricted to the bug-repair subset only.
Within the same bugs, unfixed cases show many judged-acceptable outputs but low fix conversion.
minimal_test: Generate or collect a paired dataset containing both general code-reasoning items and explicit bug-repair items for the same agent. Save all final outputs and attempt logs. Recompute the broad percent metric on the full set and then on the bug-repair subset only; on that same subset, also compute cost per bug and plausible-patch/fixed rates from identical outputs and trajectories to test denominator effects.

scope_conditions.task: code-reasoning

evidence_gap: The claims come from different task slices and do not disclose a common denominator or shared subset, preventing a direct decomposition of aggregate versus conditional performance.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because the two communities package the same external-information mechanism under different surface descriptions: text-generation work emphasizes retrieval augmentation, while mobile-device work emphasizes vision-centric adaptability and benchmarked agent completion.
mechanism: Terminology drift hides a shared idea: both lines of work try to improve decision quality by incorporating information not stored in model weights, but one names it RAG and the other frames it as multimodal agent perception over device state.
predictions: Mobile-agent papers rarely cite RAG despite using external context
Adding retrieved app/help text improves Mobile-Eval completion
minimal_test: Compare citation neighborhoods and terminology frequencies for papers citing retrieval augmentation versus mobile-agent benchmarks, then run a small Mobile-Eval replication where a vision-centric agent receives retrieved app documentation or prior-task snippets alongside screenshots and measure completion/accuracy deltas.

scope_conditions.method: retrieval augmentation and vision-centric agent pipelines
scope_conditions.task: multi-step mobile device operations with text-bearing interfaces

evidence_gap: Direct ablations isolating retrieved textual context from visual perception in mobile-agent benchmarks are missing.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because venue separation and metric priorities split the communities: on-device/mobile systems papers optimize per-token latency and deployment efficiency, while text-generation papers foreground answer quality and coverage, so each side under-reads the other's results even when the failure modes overlap.
mechanism: Methodological priors differ: systems researchers treat generation as an inference-engine problem, whereas generation researchers treat it as a knowledge/quality problem; shared mechanisms are obscured when speed and accuracy are reported on different benchmarks with incompatible success criteria.
predictions: Text-generation papers seldom report mobile-style per-token latency on phones
LLMCad-style optimizations preserve RAG quality but change end-to-end latency
minimal_test: Perform a focused literature comparison of reported metrics across the cited mobile inference and text-generation papers, then port one compact RAG baseline to a smartphone using an LLMCad-like inference engine and measure answer accuracy, coverage, and per-token latency together.

scope_conditions.method: on-device inference engine plus retrieval-augmented generation
scope_conditions.task: text generation on smartphone-class hardware

evidence_gap: Few side-by-side studies jointly report retrieval quality metrics and mobile deployment metrics for the same generation pipeline.

hypothesis: Cross-pollination may be delayed by generational lag in multimodal training assumptions: mobile-agent researchers rely on stronger task-specific agent scaffolding, while text-generation researchers often discuss incomplete or unstable outputs as a finetuning issue, leaving shared robustness tools underexplored across domains.
mechanism: The communities may inherit different default fixes for failure: mobile work adds agent structure and benchmarked task decomposition, whereas multimodal generation work adds staged finetuning; each remedy could transfer but is not yet routinely tested in the other's benchmark.
predictions: Stage-wise finetuning reduces malformed action text in mobile agents
Agent-style task decomposition lowers incomplete outputs in multimodal text generation
minimal_test: Run two small replications: first, add a second-stage finetuning protocol to a mobile-agent text/action head and measure malformed or incomplete action strings on Mobile-Eval; second, import mobile-agent style subgoal decomposition into a MiniGPT-4-like generation setup and measure completion failure rate before versus after decomposition.

scope_conditions.method: stage-wise finetuning and agentic task decomposition
scope_conditions.task: multimodal text output and mobile action-sequence generation

evidence_gap: There is little direct evidence comparing finetuning-based robustness fixes against agent-structure-based fixes across both multimodal generation and mobile-operation benchmarks.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because VQA papers frame improvements as numeric-reasoning or benchmark-specific gains, while vision-language understanding papers frame similar improvements as general alignment success, obscuring shared mechanisms.
mechanism: Terminology drift and task-label mismatch cause citation search and reviewer matching to separate work on text+image reasoning even when both communities manipulate external textual support or intermediate reasoning structure.
predictions: VQA abstracts mention 'number questions' or benchmark names more often than 'alignment' or 'understanding'.
Importing VQA-style retrieval context into a vision-language understanding benchmark improves numeric or detail-sensitive items more than open-ended ones.
minimal_test: Compare title/abstract keyword distributions and citation overlap for papers citing these claims; then run a small study adding caption retrieval context from the VQA RAG setup to MiniGPT-4-style evaluation prompts and measure gains by question subtype.

scope_conditions.method: retrieval-augmented prompting vs frozen-alignment multimodal models
scope_conditions.task: text+image QA or instruction following with numeric/detail-sensitive questions

evidence_gap: There is little direct evidence that the same item types drive gains in both communities because shared subtype evaluations are not reported side by side.

hypothesis: Venue separation may keep the disconnect alive: VQA emphasizes dataset-tuned methods like retrieval and distilled programs, whereas vision-language understanding emphasizes broad demo-style capability, so each side under-tests the other's favored tooling.
mechanism: Different evaluation cultures reward either benchmark deltas or broad qualitative success, making cross-citations low even when methods are portable.
predictions: Vision-language understanding papers report fewer controlled ablations against no-retrieval or no-program baselines than VQA papers.
Applying visual program distillation to a MiniGPT-4-style benchmark yields larger gains on compositional or counting prompts than architecture-only variants.
minimal_test: Sample matched papers from both communities and code evaluation style, ablation density, and venue; then replicate MiniGPT-4 with a lightweight visual-program distillation or chain-of-operations supervision layer on a subset of MMBench or similar prompts.

scope_conditions.method: visual program distillation transferred into aligned VLMs
scope_conditions.task: vision-language understanding benchmarks containing compositional, counting, or multi-step image questions

evidence_gap: No small published benchmark directly compares architecture tuning against distilled intermediate reasoning on the same vision-language understanding evaluation.

hypothesis: A methodological-priors gap may be responsible: VQA assumes explicit externalization of reasoning helps, while vision-language understanding often assumes stronger end-to-end alignment is sufficient, so each community discounts the other's positive results.
mechanism: Researchers with end-to-end priors may interpret retrieval or distilled programs as dataset-specific scaffolds, while VQA researchers may treat alignment-only gains as insufficiently diagnostic for reasoning failures such as counting.
predictions: Fine-tuning-only variants transfer less reliably to counting benchmarks than retrieval or program-supervised variants.
On a shared text+image benchmark, retrieval plus program supervision outperforms extra-layer tuning specifically on numeric reasoning slices.
minimal_test: Take the MiniGPT-4 baseline and compare three small interventions on a shared benchmark subset: extra layers, caption retrieval, and visual program supervision; report overall and numeric-reasoning slice performance.

scope_conditions.method: extra-layer fine-tuning vs retrieval augmentation vs distilled intermediate programs
scope_conditions.task: shared text+image benchmarks with identifiable counting/number subsets such as TallyQA-style or MMBench numeric items

evidence_gap: Current claims do not isolate whether observed gains come from explicit reasoning support or from generic scaling/alignment effects under matched data and compute.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because domain-QA papers frame the shared problem as decomposition, tool-use, and retrieval over structured evidence, while computer vision papers frame it as perception and modality alignment, so similar control-layer ideas are not recognized as the same mechanism.
mechanism: Terminology drift plus venue-specific benchmark design hides that iterative evidence selection in agentic/domain-QA systems and iterative frame or region selection in vision systems both address context allocation under limited budgets.
predictions: Cross-citations are rarer when papers use 'perception' terms instead of 'retrieval/tool-use' terms
An imported agentic control loop improves visual QA more on small-detail tasks than on coarse scene tasks
minimal_test: Run a focused literature comparison on 2023-2025 domain-QA and vision papers using shared_concepts keywords, then replicate a simple agentic retrieval/validation loop from domain-QA on Blink or TextVQA by letting the model iteratively request crops/captions before answering.

scope_conditions.method: agent or tool-use systems with explicit iterative control
scope_conditions.task: text+image QA or perception-heavy evaluation under context/compute limits

evidence_gap: Missing direct ablations that rename and transfer the same control policy across text-only QA and visual benchmarks.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because methodological priors differ: domain-QA treats numeric reasoning errors as externally correctable with executors and decomposition, whereas computer vision often treats failures as representation limits of multimodal models, reducing incentives to import symbolic tooling.
mechanism: One community assumes mistakes come from missing computation or schema grounding, the other assumes mistakes come from weak visual encoders; this leads to separate intervention styles even when both face localization-plus-reasoning bottlenecks.
predictions: Executor-style modules help vision benchmarks with counting or coordinate questions more than free-form description questions
Region/schema-linking prompts reduce errors on circled-point localization tasks relative to plain multimodal prompting
minimal_test: Import domain-QA tooling into a vision benchmark by adding a lightweight external executor plus schema-linking prompt template for coordinates/counts on Blink or MMBench subsets, and compare against the same VLM without executor support.

scope_conditions.task: numeric reasoning or localization in text+image settings

evidence_gap: Little evidence directly separates visual encoding failure from downstream reasoning/execution failure on the same examples.

hypothesis: Useful cross-pollination may be delayed by generational and venue lag: newer multimodal agent papers and newer agentic RAG papers are active in parallel but have not yet converged on shared evaluation protocols, so each community lacks a benchmark showing the other's tool as decisive.
mechanism: High recent activity with balanced impact suggests active but separate optimization loops; without a common benchmark, improvements like graph retrieval in QA and specialist perception modules in vision remain locally validated only.
predictions: Graph-based retrieval from domain-QA improves multimodal QA when evidence spans multiple text and image snippets
Specialist perception modules from vision improve domain-QA on chart/document/image-grounded questions more than on pure text QA
minimal_test: Two small transfer studies: (1) replace vanilla retrieval in CrisisMMD-style multimodal QA with GNN-style evidence retrieval; (2) add a specialist detector/cropper pipeline before answering on TAT-DQA or similar image-grounded QA and measure EM/accuracy changes.

scope_conditions.method: graph retrieval on one side, specialist perception/cropping on the other
scope_conditions.task: multihop multimodal QA or document/chart/image-grounded QA

evidence_gap: No head-to-head benchmark currently tests both graph retrieval and specialist perception under the same evaluation protocol.

hypothesis: The contradiction is driven by dataset composition: long-context methods look strong on synthetic or needle-style retrieval benchmarks but weaken on document-grounded, domain-specific QA with multi-span reasoning.
mechanism: Needle-in-haystack and key-retrieval tasks mainly test positional recall of a single salient fact, whereas datasets such as CUAD require sustained reasoning over noisy legal text and multiple relevant spans; increasing context window helps the former more than the latter, flipping the observed effect of long-context support.
predictions: Needle-style gains shrink on CUAD-like QA
Within one model, recall stays high while answer accuracy drops
minimal_test: Evaluate the same long-context models and prompt on two matched suites with identical context lengths: one synthetic/needle retrieval set and one real document QA set (e.g., legal/contracts), then compare whether the positive-vs-negative discrepancy disappears when task composition is held constant.

scope_conditions.task: long-context-QA

evidence_gap: Current claims mix synthetic retrieval, long-context recall, and real document QA without a controlled cross-dataset comparison at fixed lengths.

hypothesis: The disagreement arises because prompt format moderates long-context performance: simple retrieval-style prompts amplify apparent benefits, while instruction-heavy or compressed prompts introduce salience loss that can reverse gains.
mechanism: Models can succeed when the prompt asks for exact recall from long context, but prompts that compress, summarize, or embed multiple instructions compete for attention and reduce effective access to distant evidence; this can make long-context support look only partially helpful or even costly.
predictions: Direct quote prompts outperform compressed prompts at 64K+
Prompt compression hurts far-span evidence more than near-span evidence
minimal_test: Run the same model, dataset, and decoding setting with three prompt templates only: direct retrieval, instruction-heavy QA, and compressed-context QA. If claim differences shrink after fixing prompt format, the contradiction was prompt-mediated.

scope_conditions.task: long-context-QA

evidence_gap: The cited papers do not report standardized prompt templates or isolate compression effects from context-window effects.

hypothesis: The conflict is partly an evaluation-protocol mismatch: some papers measure context-window capacity or recall-at-length, while others judge end-task answer accuracy or practical usability under token-limit constraints.
mechanism: A model can accept 128K-2M tokens and retrieve a planted fact, yet still fail to improve end-to-end QA accuracy because generation, reasoning, or truncation policies differ; conversely, papers emphasizing token-limit exclusions or answer accuracy can report negative results even when raw recall is strong.
predictions: Capacity claims correlate weakly with answer accuracy
After harmonizing metric and truncation rules, effect signs align more often
minimal_test: Re-score the same model outputs under a unified protocol: fixed effective context length, identical truncation policy, and both recall and answer-accuracy metrics reported side by side. Check whether the reported contradiction disappears once evaluation criteria are standardized.

scope_conditions.task: long-context-QA

evidence_gap: The available claims conflate maximum supported tokens, recall, and task accuracy, with little evidence from shared evaluation scripts or common metrics.

hypothesis: The conflicting long-context-QA results are primarily driven by the absolute context_length regime, with gains appearing at medium-long windows but degrading or saturating at very short or ultra-long windows.
mechanism: As context_length increases, models first benefit from fitting all evidence, but beyond a model-specific range attention dilution, position bias, and optimization mismatch reduce effective retrieval and answer accuracy.
predictions: Performance peaks at an intermediate context window on at least one benchmark.
A 16K-cap model underperforms or fails once the same benchmark is expanded beyond its limit.
minimal_test: On one original benchmark such as the synthetic key retrieval task from arxiv:2308.12950#c01 or CUAD from arxiv:2407.11005#c03, hold model, prompt, metric, and data fixed while sweeping only context_length across 8K, 16K, 32K, 64K, 128K, and max-supported values; measure accuracy/recall and fit a non-monotonic curve.

scope_conditions.task: long-context-QA

evidence_gap: The current claims mix capacity-limit statements with quality statements, so matched benchmark curves across multiple window sizes are missing.

hypothesis: The divergence is caused by whether context_length is measured as total input budget versus effective evidence-bearing length, so papers using the same nominal window are not testing the same setting.
mechanism: Formatting overhead, instructions, multimodal tokens, and distractor content consume part of the window; when effective evidence-bearing tokens are held constant, apparent contradictions across 16K, 128K, and >2M reports should shrink.
predictions: Equal nominal windows with different overhead produce different recall.
Normalizing for usable evidence tokens reduces the reported advantage of larger windows.
minimal_test: On MileBench/MMNeedle or a needle-in-haystack benchmark, hold model, task, benchmark items, and answer format fixed while sweeping only context_length by varying overhead-controlled windows: e.g. 32K, 64K, 128K total tokens with matched evidence payload fractions versus unmatched fractions; compare recall at each total length.

scope_conditions.task: long-context-QA

evidence_gap: The claims do not report how much of each long context is actual evidence versus prompt or modality overhead, preventing apples-to-apples comparison.

hypothesis: The responsible setting variable is context_length relative to answer-span position, with long-context helping when the target evidence lies deep in the sequence but not when the answer is near the front or heavily diluted.
mechanism: Transformer retrieval quality depends on positional distance; increasing context_length only helps if the benchmark places relevant information beyond shorter-window reach, whereas near-start answers or dense distractor settings may show little gain or even worse accuracy.
predictions: Longer windows help more for answer spans placed in the last quartile than the first quartile.
At fixed task and model, extending context without moving the answer deeper gives little or no accuracy gain.
minimal_test: On the original synthetic key retrieval task from arxiv:2308.12950#c01 or a needle-in-haystack setup related to arxiv:2501.00663#c02, hold model, corpus, prompt, and metric fixed while sweeping only context_length and placing the answer at proportional positions within each length; compare front/middle/end retrieval accuracy across 16K, 32K, 64K, and 128K.

scope_conditions.task: long-context-QA

evidence_gap: The cited claims report window size and overall recall/accuracy but not matched answer-position distributions, so positional confounding remains unresolved.

hypothesis: A terminology and task-framing drift persists because remote sensing papers cast the problem as grounding/segmentation with scale-aware evaluation, while biology papers cast it as trait/species QA or biological reasoning, obscuring that both are text+image modality-alignment problems with attribute-vs-category failure modes.
mechanism: Different labels for closely related capabilities cause search and citation mismatch: remote sensing emphasizes gIoU/cIoU, grounding, and hierarchical refinement, whereas biology emphasizes trait identification, species classification, and reasoning accuracy, so overlapping findings are not indexed as comparable.
predictions: Biology papers rarely cite grounding or segmentation terms despite discussing trait localization implicitly
Remote sensing papers rarely discuss attribute-vs-category gaps even when local detail is central
minimal_test: Run a focused keyword-and-citation comparison on the cited papers and their references, then replicate one biology trait-identification benchmark by adding a remote-sensing-style region grounding or multi-scale localization intermediate step before answering.

scope_conditions.method: multimodal text+image models without domain-specific pretraining changes
scope_conditions.task: fine-grained recognition or reasoning tasks where local visual evidence matters more than single-label classification

evidence_gap: Missing direct ablations showing whether localized intermediate supervision transfers across the two task taxonomies.

hypothesis: Venue separation and methodological priors persist because remote sensing rewards benchmark construction plus supervised adaptation, while biology currently explores prompting and RL-style reasoning gains, so each community undervalues the other's strongest tooling despite balanced recent activity.
mechanism: Remote sensing papers center dataset curation and fine-tuning pipelines, while biology papers center backbone scaling and GRPO-like refinement; reviewers and authors therefore treat the other's methods as out-of-scope rather than as transferable components.
predictions: Remote sensing benchmark papers will contain more dataset-construction and SFT baselines than RL baselines
Biology reasoning papers will contain more scaling/RL ablations than localization or instruction-dataset curation ablations
minimal_test: Compare ablation tables across the two literatures for presence of SFT, dataset curation, RL, and scaling analyses; then import GRPO from the biology setup into a remote-sensing reasoning benchmark with fixed visual encoder and measure gains over SFT-only baselines.

scope_conditions.method: instruction-tuned multimodal or hybrid models with trainable language-side adaptation
scope_conditions.task: reasoning-oriented benchmarks with enough labeled reward signal to support preference or outcome optimization

evidence_gap: No small controlled study yet isolates whether RL refinement adds value in remote sensing beyond the already reported SFT and retrieval gains.

hypothesis: Cross-pollination would be most productive if biology imported remote sensing’s retrieval and hierarchical multi-scale evidence aggregation, while remote sensing imported biology’s scaling-aware RL refinement, because both communities report gains when models receive better structured context but test this under different names.
mechanism: In biology, species-classification failures and mixed prompting effects suggest missing localized evidence selection; in remote sensing, strong SFT/RAG gains suggest latent headroom from post-SFT reasoning optimization similar to GRPO, especially on multi-step spatial queries.
predictions: RAG plus hierarchical region selection will improve biology species-classification more than trait-identification accuracy
GRPO after SFT will help larger remote-sensing VLM backbones more than smaller ones on reasoning splits
minimal_test: Two small replications: add remote-sensing-style RAG and hierarchical refinement to a biological image QA/species benchmark, and add GRPO on top of an existing remote-sensing SFT benchmark while varying backbone size; compare against each field’s standard baseline.

scope_conditions.method: text+image systems where external retrieval or region proposals can be inserted without rebuilding the encoder
scope_conditions.task: benchmarks with explicit reasoning prompts and separable fine-grained versus coarse-grained evaluation slices

evidence_gap: The literature lacks direct import studies showing asymmetry of transfer—whether retrieval/localization helps biology more than RL, and whether RL helps remote sensing more than extra retrieval.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because scientific-research QA papers operationalize reliability at the claim-and-citation level, while programming papers operationalize it at execution or string-match level, so each community may not recognize the other's evaluation protocol as relevant despite both studying text-grounded reliability risk.
mechanism: Terminology drift around evaluation protocol and reliability risk causes metric incompatibility: claim recognition/accuracy/source citation in scientific research versus pass@k/BLEU/exact match in programming, which can hide the shared concern of whether generated text is supported and correct.
predictions: Code-generation papers rarely mention claim-level citation metrics
Applying claim-accuracy annotation to code explanations reorders model rankings
minimal_test: Sample 30 papers from each community and code metric vocab overlap; then run a small study on MBPP or APPS explanations where outputs are scored with claim recognition, claim accuracy, and source citation frequency alongside pass@k.

scope_conditions.task: evaluation and code-generation

evidence_gap: There is little direct evidence on whether claim-level factual metrics correlate with or diverge from pass@k-style code metrics on the same outputs.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because venue separation and methodological priors steer scientific-research systems toward retrieval-grounded QA and programming systems toward pretrained/fine-tuned generation, even when both rely on text as the main interface; cross-pollination would look like importing RAG-style source attribution into programming benchmarks and importing program-aided verification into scientific research QA.
mechanism: Different default toolchains create separate benchmark cultures: RAG papers emphasize retrieved evidence and citation frequency, whereas code-generation papers emphasize model scaling and benchmark execution, reducing mutual citation even when both aim to reduce unsupported generations.
predictions: RAG with documentation retrieval improves code benchmark faithfulness without large pass@k gains
Program-aided checking lowers scientific QA hallucination frequency
minimal_test: Add retrieval over official library docs or problem statements to a code-generation benchmark and score citation/usefulness; separately, convert a subset of scientific-research claims into executable or rule-checkable predicates using PAL-style verification and compare hallucination frequency before versus after verification.

scope_conditions.task: domain-QA, code-generation, math-reasoning

evidence_gap: Missing are head-to-head studies that measure whether retrieval-based attribution and program-aided verification transfer across these domains without major task redesign.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because a generational lag separates newer scientific-research reliability benchmarks built around small targeted question sets from older programming benchmarks centered on broad multilingual performance, so the communities may not yet have aligned on a shared text-centric failure taxonomy; cross-pollination would look like importing multilingual benchmark stratification from programming into scientific research evaluation and importing fine-grained hallucination/source-citation auditing from scientific research into programming.
mechanism: Benchmark design priors differ: one side uses compact manually targeted evaluations with explicit claims, while the other uses larger benchmark suites with language/task stratification, leading to different notions of generalization and masking analogous failure modes.
predictions: Scientific QA scores vary more by subdomain when stratified like MultiPL-E languages
Programming benchmark outputs show measurable unsupported-assertion rates despite strong pass@k
minimal_test: Create stratified subsets of the 50-question scientific benchmark by source or claim type and compare variance patterns to MultiPL-E language slices; annotate a small sample of programming outputs for unsupported assertions and missing source grounding using the scientific-research audit protocol.

scope_conditions.task: evaluation, domain-QA, code-generation

evidence_gap: We lack comparative studies on whether benchmark stratification and claim-level audits reveal the same stability or failure patterns across these two communities.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because both communities study failure propagation in agentic multimodal systems but package it under different evaluation vocabularies, so shared mechanisms are obscured by terminology drift.
mechanism: Safety papers frame round-based prompt spread as jailbreak infection, while mobile-device papers frame similar sequential failures as task completion or adaptability; this shifts keywords, baselines, and metrics enough to suppress mutual retrieval and citation despite overlapping evaluation protocol structure.
predictions: Safety papers rarely cite Mobile-Eval-style benchmarks despite shared agentic multimodal setup
Metric names will differ more than task mechanics in a side-by-side corpus sample
minimal_test: Run a focused literature comparison on 20 safety and 20 mobile-agent papers, coding task graph structure, modality use, and metric names; test whether shared protocol features cluster more strongly than shared terminology, then re-index abstracts with normalized vocabulary to estimate missing cross-citations.

scope_conditions.method: agent-based evaluation with text or text+image inputs
scope_conditions.task: multi-step mobile operations or prompt/jailbreak propagation

evidence_gap: We lack a coded comparison showing whether keyword mismatch rather than substantive task difference drives the citation gap.

hypothesis: Cross-pollination may be limited by venue separation and methodological priors: safety emphasizes adversarial robustness and attack surfaces, whereas mobile-device work emphasizes completion efficiency and environment adaptability, so each community under-measures the other's main failure mode.
mechanism: Researchers optimize for different benchmark incentives; safety imports red teaming, corruption tests, and infection dynamics, while mobile benchmarks reward accuracy/completion under normal use, leading to asymmetric reporting and few directly comparable results.
predictions: Importing image-corruption and attack-injection tests into Mobile-Eval will lower completion rates on multi-app tasks
Applying mobile-style completion metrics to prompt-infection studies will reveal attack cases that still finish user tasks
minimal_test: Take a mobile-agent benchmark with text+image instructions and add two safety-style perturbations: prompt injection hidden in on-screen text and image corruptions such as resize/JPEG; separately, score a prompt-infection benchmark with mobile-style task completion and step-success metrics to see whether robustness and utility decouple.

scope_conditions.task: screen-based action selection and instruction following

evidence_gap: There is little evidence from shared benchmarks that jointly report normal-use completion and adversarial robustness for the same multimodal agent.

hypothesis: A productive bridge would be bidirectional tooling transfer: safety's adversarial fine-tuning and infection-style network evaluation could help mobile agents, while mobile-device benchmarking could help safety quantify real-world utility costs of defenses; the disconnect may persist due to generational lag between fast-moving safety debates and younger mobile-agent benchmarks.
mechanism: Safety has mature intervention tools for hardening agent behavior, but mobile work has more concrete embodied evaluation loops; because the fields matured around different timelines and artifacts, reusable tooling has not yet been systematically ported.
predictions: Adversarial fine-tuning with mobile-specific negative trajectories will improve resistance to malicious UI content without fully preserving completion
Safety defenses evaluated on Mobile-Eval-like tasks will show measurable robustness-utility tradeoffs invisible in refusal-only metrics
minimal_test: Small replication study: construct a set of malicious mobile UI trajectories and on-screen prompt-injection examples, fine-tune a mobile agent with safety-style negative samples, then evaluate on Mobile-Eval plus adversarial screens; in the reverse direction, evaluate a safety-aligned multimodal agent on benchmarked multi-app tasks to measure utility loss from defenses.

scope_conditions.task: mobile device operations and adversarial instruction handling

evidence_gap: Missing are controlled studies that transfer safety hardening methods into mobile agents and report both attack resistance and task completion on the same benchmark.

hypothesis: The sign of fine-tuning's safety effect flips with safety dataset composition: adversarial negative samples improve robustness, while broad synthetic or web-filtered data mainly attenuate toxicity and can look mixed under other safety endpoints.
mechanism: Fine-tuning on explicitly adversarial failure cases teaches refusal and interference-resistant behaviors that transfer to agentic attacks, whereas data curation that removes toxic web content mostly shifts surface generation style; if papers measure different safety subdomains, the same umbrella method can appear strongly positive or only mixed.
predictions: Adversarial-only fine-tuning helps agentic attack robustness more than toxicity scores.
Web-filtered/synthetic data helps toxicity scores more than tool-chain robustness.
minimal_test: Evaluate one base model under two matched fine-tuning datasets: (a) adversarial tool-chain negatives and (b) web-filtered/textbook-like synthetic data, then score both on the same toxicity/bias suite and the same adversarial tool-use robustness suite; if the contradiction is due to dataset composition, effect directions should align once the dataset type is held fixed across papers.

scope_conditions.task: safety across toxicity/bias and adversarial robustness

evidence_gap: The current claims do not report a common base model, common fine-tuning budget, or cross-evaluation on both safety subdomains.

hypothesis: The sign flips with model scale and capability regime: smaller models show mixed safety gains from curated fine-tuning, while stronger or more agentic models benefit more clearly from adversarial alignment fine-tuning.
mechanism: Limited-capacity models may trade off helpfulness and refusal calibration when fine-tuned on curated safety data, producing only attenuated or mixed reductions in toxic behavior, whereas higher-capability models can absorb adversarial fine-tuning signals without large utility loss and thus show clearer robustness gains.
predictions: Smaller models show weaker or mixed safety gains under the same fine-tuning recipe.
Larger/agentic models show clearer robustness gains from adversarial negatives.
minimal_test: Run the same safety fine-tuning recipe and same evaluation protocol on a size-matched family of models including phi-1.5-scale and a larger successor; hold data, steps, and prompts fixed, and test whether the observed safety effect changes sign by scale but converges when model size is matched across comparisons.

scope_conditions.task: safety; especially phi-1.5-style toxicity reduction versus agentic robustness

evidence_gap: One claim names phi-1.5 while the other does not specify model identity or scale, preventing direct capability-matched comparison.

hypothesis: The sign flips with evaluation protocol and prompt format: open-ended toxicity probes can yield mixed apparent gains, whereas structured adversarial tool-chain prompts reveal stronger benefits from fine-tuning.
mechanism: Safety fine-tuning may primarily improve behavior under specific prompt distributions—e.g., multi-turn, tool-mediated, attack-like interactions—while having smaller or noisier effects on short-form toxicity prompts; differing prompt templates, jailbreak framing, and scoring rules can therefore reverse whether the method appears beneficial.
predictions: Under identical prompts and metrics, disagreement shrinks substantially.
Multi-turn tool-use prompts show larger gains than single-turn toxicity prompts.
minimal_test: Re-evaluate the fine-tuned systems from both papers using a unified protocol: same prompt templates, same decoding settings, same annotator/model-judge rubric, and matched single-turn versus multi-turn attack prompts; if protocol differences drive the contradiction, the sign discrepancy should diminish when prompt format and scoring are fixed.

scope_conditions.task: safety evaluation across toxicity and adversarial interaction

evidence_gap: The claims provide no shared benchmark, decoding configuration, or common scoring procedure, so current evidence cannot separate true method differences from protocol-induced sign changes.

graph_bridge.from: tool-use robustness under adversarial long-horizon tool chains

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because both communities study LLM-mediated text evaluation but describe it with different local goals—regulatory compliance frames it as accept/reject or similarity-to-reference assessment, while business frames it as strategic agent interaction—so shared evaluation protocol work is not retrieved by keyword search.
mechanism: Terminology drift plus task-label mismatch can hide overlap: papers indexed under compliance QA, BertScore, and accept/reject evaluation are not easily linked to business simulation papers indexed under competition dynamics or agent interactions, even when all rely on LLM judgments over text outputs.
predictions: Compliance papers will overuse evaluation terms while business papers overuse agent/simulation terms
A joint keyword search on GPT-4 text evaluation retrieves more compliance than business papers

scope_conditions.method: LLM-based evaluation and GPT-4 agent simulation

Importing compliance metrics into a business agent benchmark will rank prompt variants differently than narrative qualitative assessment
minimal_test: Take a small business-agent text corpus from a benchmark like restaurant-customer interactions and score agent outputs using compliance-style BertScore, context recall, and accept/reject rubrics; compare those rankings to the original business evaluation conclusions.

scope_conditions.task: agentic business dialogue with textual outputs

evidence_gap: We still lack side-by-side replications showing whether benchmark-style metrics preserve or overturn business-community model rankings.

hypothesis: The most useful cross-pollination may be bidirectional: business should import compliance-style LLM evaluator calibration and retrieval/input ablations, while compliance should import multi-agent scenario testing from business to stress-test whether high agreement metrics survive strategic, interactive settings.
mechanism: The shared mechanism could be robustness of text evaluation under changing context: compliance has stronger tools for controlled agreement measurement, and business has stronger tools for generating adversarial or strategic textual situations that reveal hidden evaluator failures.

Retrieval/input ablations from compliance will measurably change outcome judgments in business simulations
minimal_test: Small replication study with two imports: first, apply GPT-4 accept/reject evaluation and context-recall ablations to business-agent transcripts; second, convert a compliance QA benchmark into a multi-agent interaction setting and measure whether evaluator-human agreement and model rankings remain stable.

scope_conditions.task: text-based evaluation under static versus interactive conditions

evidence_gap: Missing are controlled studies that hold model family constant while varying only interaction structure and evaluation protocol across the two communities.

hypothesis: The sign of chain-of-thought effects flips with dataset composition, especially whether benchmarks reward decomposable stepwise reasoning versus tightly interdependent or ambiguity-sensitive reasoning.
mechanism: On tasks whose latent solution can be factorized into mostly independent substeps, explicit intermediate reasoning scaffolds search and raises accuracy; on datasets with high variable interdependence, misleading cues, or ethical ambiguity, longer chains amplify early mistakes and consistency failures, turning gains into losses.
predictions: CoT gains are positive on low-interdependence subsets
CoT gains vanish or reverse on high-interdependence subsets
minimal_test: Construct matched evaluation slices within one benchmark family that differ only in interdependence/compositional depth while keeping model, prompt template, decoding, and scorer fixed; compare direct-answer versus CoT deltas on each slice and check whether papers' contradictory signs disappear after conditioning on slice type.

scope_conditions.task: multi-step-reasoning

evidence_gap: Most claims do not report a common decomposition of item difficulty, interdependence, or ambiguity across datasets, so cross-paper sign changes cannot yet be normalized by composition.

hypothesis: The contradiction is driven by prompt format differences—zero-shot trigger phrases, few-shot exemplars, and rationale structure can switch CoT from helpful guidance to harmful prompt overconstraint.
mechanism: CoT performance is highly sensitive to whether prompts use zero-shot 'think step by step', 2-shot/3-shot exemplars, customized reasoning templates, or multimodal staged instructions; good formats elicit latent reasoning, while mismatched exemplars or verbose templates consume context, bias solution paths, or induce imitation of wrong reasoning, reversing the effect relative to direct answers.
predictions: A single standardized CoT template reduces between-paper variance
Reordering or replacing exemplars changes the CoT delta sign
minimal_test: Run the same model-task pairs from the conflicting papers under a factorial prompt ablation: direct answer, zero-shot CoT trigger, fixed 2-shot CoT exemplars, fixed 3-shot CoT exemplars, and the paper-specific customized template; keep dataset, decoding, and evaluation fixed, then test whether contradiction disappears once prompt format is held constant.

scope_conditions.task: multi-step-reasoning

evidence_gap: Conflicting papers rarely release fully matched prompt text, exemplar order, or rationale formatting, preventing direct attribution of sign changes to prompt structure alone.

hypothesis: Model scale and reasoning-tuning level moderate CoT effectiveness, with larger or reasoning-specialized models benefiting while smaller or differently aligned models are harmed by forced intermediate reasoning.
mechanism: Generating useful chains requires enough capacity and training to maintain coherent latent state across steps; larger or reasoning-tuned models convert extra tokens into better search, whereas smaller or multimodal models may overfit to spurious rationale patterns, lose answer calibration, or incur exposure to compounding errors, making direct answers better.
predictions: CoT advantage increases with model scale within one family
Small or untuned models show neutral/negative CoT deltas
minimal_test: Evaluate direct-answer and identical CoT prompts across a same-family size ladder or tuning ladder (e.g., mini/8B/large or base/reasoning-tuned) on one shared benchmark with fixed decoding and scoring; if the contradiction collapses into a monotonic scale-dependent pattern, scale is the moderator.

scope_conditions.task: multi-step-reasoning

evidence_gap: Current claims compare heterogeneous model families and multimodal stacks, so scale is confounded with architecture, training mixture, and instruction tuning.

hypothesis: The contradiction is moderated by safety dataset composition: RLHF appears beneficial on refusal/jailbreak-heavy safety data but can worsen or reverse behavior on socially sensitive decision scenarios like admissions fairness.
mechanism: Preference data dominated by harmful-request refusal teaches conservative abstention and anti-jailbreak behavior, while fairness-sensitive allocation tasks require calibrated tradeoffs among protected attributes; RLHF optimized on one mixture can shift responses in a direction that looks safer on jailbreak benchmarks yet produces biased outcomes on admissions cases.
predictions: Matching safety data mixtures reduces disagreement.
Fairness-heavy RLHF weakens jailbreak gains.
minimal_test: Train or evaluate the same base model with RLHF under two controlled preference-data mixtures: one refusal/jailbreak-heavy and one fairness-decision-heavy, then measure both jailbreak resistance and admissions discrimination with identical prompts and evaluator settings; if the contradiction is due to composition, effect signs should align once both papers use the same mixture.

scope_conditions.task: safety

evidence_gap: The claims do not report comparable annotation distributions or the relative share of jailbreak-refusal versus fairness-sensitive examples in the RLHF data.

hypothesis: The contradiction is moderated by prompt format and task framing: RLHF helps when prompts are direct harmful-request or jailbreak prompts, but can flip behavior when the prompt embeds identity attributes in a comparative reasoning template such as Q+IF admissions decisions.
mechanism: RLHF often reinforces surface-level instruction following and refusal heuristics; in direct safety prompts this increases safe compliance, whereas in structured comparative prompts with demographic cues it may amplify framing-sensitive shortcuts or overcorrection, producing mixed fairness outcomes as training proceeds.
predictions: Reformatting admissions prompts changes RLHF effect sign.
Standardized prompt templates shrink claim differences.
minimal_test: Use one model and one RLHF checkpoint family, then evaluate on the same underlying examples under multiple prompt templates: direct harmful-request, neutral safety QA, and Q+IF comparative admissions prompts with controlled identity mentions; if prompt framing is the moderator, the sign difference should disappear or systematically move when both papers' tasks are rendered in a shared template.

scope_conditions.task: safety

evidence_gap: The reported claims do not isolate whether observed changes come from RLHF itself or from template-specific sensitivity to identity-laden reasoning prompts.

hypothesis: The contradiction is moderated by model scale and RLHF training intensity: RLHF yields monotonic safety gains for chat-tuned models at one scale but causes non-monotonic or sign-flipping behavior in very large models as the number of RLHF steps increases.
mechanism: Larger models can over-optimize reward proxies during extended RLHF, leading to overshooting or behavioral drift on fairness-related subdomains even while broad safety and jailbreak metrics improve; a smaller or differently initialized chat model may remain in the beneficial regime.
predictions: Step-matched comparisons reduce the contradiction.
Large models show more non-monotonic fairness curves.
minimal_test: Construct a step-matched grid across model scales and RLHF intensities using comparable pre-RLHF chat/instruction tuning, then evaluate identical safety and fairness benchmarks at fixed checkpoints (e.g., 0, 50, 200, 1000 steps); if scale-by-intensity is the moderator, contradictory signs should collapse when comparing the same scale and training step regime.

scope_conditions.task: safety

evidence_gap: The claims differ simultaneously in model family and apparent RLHF dosage, but do not provide matched checkpoint trajectories across scales.

hypothesis: The inconsistency arises because exact-match/accuracy measure answer key equivalence, while text-similarity metrics reward semantic overlap even when the answer is not the canonical gold string.
mechanism: On factual-QA, models often produce paraphrases, longer explanatory answers, or partially correct spans; similarity metrics can score these outputs high, whereas exact match and strict accuracy count them wrong unless they match the reference answer form or accepted alias exactly.
predictions: Paraphrased correct answers gain similarity but lose EM.
Model ranking changes more under similarity than accuracy.
minimal_test: Collect a shared factual-QA set with gold answers and run the same model outputs from evaluation-method once per question. Preserve raw generations. Then score those identical outputs with both exact match/accuracy and a text-similarity metric suite on the same examples; manually bucket outputs into exact canonical answers, paraphrases, and partially correct explanations to test whether the divergence is concentrated in the paraphrase bucket.

scope_conditions.task: factual-QA

evidence_gap: Missing paired per-example scores for the same generations under both strict and similarity-based metrics, plus alias-normalization details.

hypothesis: The metrics diverge because similarity metrics are less sensitive to unsupported hedging or extra false content, whereas exact match/accuracy for factual-QA typically require a single unambiguous correct answer.
mechanism: A model can answer with the right entity embedded inside verbose text or mixed with incorrect qualifiers; similarity remains high due to lexical/semantic proximity to the reference, but exact match and accuracy mark the response wrong when any non-canonical or contradictory content is present.
predictions: Verbose mixed answers score higher on similarity than EM.
Short constrained decoding narrows the metric gap.
minimal_test: Using one shared factual-QA eval set, generate two output variants from the same model for each question: default free-form answers and constrained short-answer outputs. Cross-score every output with both text-similarity metrics and exact match/accuracy on the same items, then compare how often free-form generations with extra clauses flip from pass under similarity to fail under EM/accuracy.

scope_conditions.task: factual-QA

evidence_gap: Need example-level annotations of verbosity, hedging, and contradiction within answers to show that extra content specifically drives disagreement.

hypothesis: Different metrics are emphasizing different latent targets: accuracy/exact match measure discrete factual identification, while text-similarity metrics partially measure style and reference affinity, making them more forgiving to answer-format mismatch and less diagnostic of factual correctness.
mechanism: If references are phrased in a particular style or include longer answer text, similarity metrics can favor outputs that resemble that phrasing even when the key fact is incomplete, while exact match/accuracy focus on whether the target fact was identified exactly enough to satisfy the answer key.
predictions: Reference-style rewriting changes similarity more than accuracy.
Alias-expanded accuracy reduces but does not erase disagreement.
minimal_test: Take a shared factual-QA evaluation set and obtain one fixed set of model generations from evaluation-method. Create paired reference variants for the same questions: original references and style-rewritten references with preserved facts, plus an alias-expanded answer key. Score the same outputs with text-similarity metrics and with exact match/accuracy against both reference versions to test whether similarity is more reference-form dependent while accuracy changes mainly with alias coverage.

scope_conditions.task: factual-QA

evidence_gap: Need controlled alternate references and alias lists for the same questions to separate factual correctness from reference-style dependence.

A DNA- or biology-inspired multimodal hybrid encoder improves domain-QA tasks requiring non-text evidence relative to text-only baselines.

predictions: Cross-citation rates rise when searching for 'evaluation protocol' rather than domain-specific metrics.
BertScore-like semantic ranking will reorder some code models relative to pass@k on MBPP or APPS.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because venue and benchmark separation makes each field treat the other's text artifacts as out-of-scope, even though both rely on contextual generation evaluated against references.
mechanism: Methodological priors reinforced by venue separation: programming papers prioritize executable correctness on APPS/MBPP/MultiPL-E, while compliance work prioritizes answer similarity and retrieval/context recall, so each community discounts the other's benchmark as insufficiently faithful to its end task.

Program-aided verification from coding will improve reliability of regulatory answers without improving BertScore much.

hypothesis: The contradiction is moderated by dataset difficulty composition: prompting helps on routine or mid-difficulty math items but can hurt on the hardest items, flipping the average effect when benchmarks contain more difficult problems.
mechanism: Prompts that scaffold outputs or add demonstrations reduce search errors on solvable items, but on very hard problems they can anchor the model to brittle reasoning patterns or longer incorrect trajectories, so datasets with more Level-5/MATH-hard items show smaller or negative gains.
predictions: Gains shrink monotonically with problem difficulty.
Difficulty-matched subsets reconcile the reported sign.
minimal_test: Re-evaluate the same prompting variants on a shared pool stratified by difficulty (e.g., GSM8K, MATH Levels 1-5, or equivalent hardness bins), then compare prompted vs unprompted accuracy within each bin and after reweighting each paper's test set to the same difficulty distribution; if the contradiction disappears under matched difficulty mix, the moderator is supported.

hypothesis: The contradiction is moderated by prompt format: output-space shaping or targeted reasoning prompts improve math performance, whereas demonstration-heavy in-context prompts can degrade it, especially when papers collapse these under the broad label of prompting.
mechanism: Different prompt formats alter the model's solution policy in opposite directions: constrained answer formats reduce invalid outputs and enforce useful structure, while demonstrations may induce imitation of irrelevant reasoning patterns, distract from the target problem, or overload context, producing net harm.
predictions: Shaping remains positive when demonstrations are removed.
Demonstration prompts turn neutral or negative under the same evaluator.
minimal_test: Implement three prompt conditions on the same models and datasets: no prompt augmentation, output-space shaping, and matched-length demonstrations, holding model, decoding, and evaluation fixed; if shaping is positive while demonstrations are negative on the same items, the cross-paper contradiction is explained by prompt-format heterogeneity rather than a single prompting effect.

hypothesis: The contradiction is moderated by model scale: prompting benefits smaller or mid-sized models but can become neutral or harmful for larger models, so papers using different scale ranges can report opposite effects.
mechanism: Smaller models may need external structure to organize reasoning, whereas larger models already possess strong latent policies and are more susceptible to prompt-induced overthinking, spurious demonstration mimicry, or calibration drift; averaging across different scale bands can therefore flip the sign.

Large-model degradation disappears when comparing only smaller models.

hypothesis: The conflicting results arise because one metric is final-answer correctness on the original problem while the other percent metric is the share of items whose correctness flips under prompting, so prompting can improve net accuracy yet also increase the percentage of newly wrong answers.
mechanism: Accuracy aggregates wins and losses into one net score, whereas a flip-rate percent isolates instability and counts regressions even when they are outweighed by more corrected items; larger models may show higher regression percent despite positive average accuracy gains.
predictions: High flip-to-wrong percent can co-occur with positive net accuracy gain.
Items changed by prompting will have lower correctness than unchanged items.
minimal_test: On one shared math-reasoning eval set, run the same base model with and without the prompting variant on every item, store paired outputs, score both output sets for final-answer accuracy, then compute on the same paired records the percent of examples that changed from correct->incorrect and incorrect->correct; compare whether methods with positive net accuracy also show substantial regression percent.

hypothesis: The mismatch comes from percent being measured over a subset such as demonstrations-affected or attempted items, while accuracy is measured over the full benchmark, so prompting can look harmful on the conditioned subset but beneficial overall.
mechanism: Subset-conditioned percentages reweight the evaluation toward hard or intervention-sensitive questions; if prompting mostly changes difficult items, the percent of harmed cases within that subset can be high even while full-set accuracy rises because many untouched easy items remain correct and some hard items improve.
predictions: Subset-conditioned percent will be worse than full-set accuracy delta.
Untouched items will dilute negative subset effects in overall accuracy.
minimal_test: Using one shared eval set, generate outputs from the same model with and without the prompting method, then define the paired subset as items whose output changed or items receiving demonstrations/extra reasoning tokens; score the same outputs two ways: full-benchmark accuracy and percent harmful within the subset, and test whether conclusions reverse between subset and full-set scoring.
